criteria.title: Non-text Content
criteria.officialText: All non-text content that is presented to the user has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose, except for the situations listed below.Controls, Input: If non-text content is a control or accepts user input, then it has a name that describes its purpose. (Refer to Success Criterion 4.1.2 for additional requirements for controls and content that accepts user input.)Time-Based Media: If non-text content is time-based media, then text alternatives at least provide descriptive identification of the non-text content. (Refer to Guideline 1.2 for additional requirements for media.)Test: If non-text content is a test or exercise that would be invalid if presented in text, then text alternatives at least provide descriptive identification of the non-text content.Sensory: If non-text content is primarily intended to create a specific sensory experience, then text alternatives at least provide descriptive identification of the non-text content.CAPTCHA: If the purpose of non-text content is to confirm that content is being accessed by a person rather than a computer, then text alternatives that identify and describe the purpose of the non-text content are provided, and alternative forms of CAPTCHA using output modes for different types of sensory perception are provided to accommodate different disabilities.Decoration, Formatting, Invisible: If non-text content is pure decoration, is used only for visual formatting, or is not presented to users, then it is implemented in a way that it can be ignored by assistive technology.
criteria.plainEnglish: Every image, icon, chart, or other non-text element needs a text alternative that conveys the same meaning. Informative images get descriptive alt text; purely decorative ones get an empty alt (alt="") so screen readers skip them; icon buttons and controls need an accessible name. The goal is that someone who can't see the visual still gets the equivalent information or function.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users—including the roughly 250 million people worldwide with significant vision loss—get nothing useful when an image has no alt text, or hear a meaningless filename like "IMG_4032.jpg" read aloud.
criteria.quickCheck: In Chrome DevTools, inspect each and icon button in the Elements panel and confirm it has meaningful alt text or an aria-label, or run the axe DevTools extension, which flags image-alt violations instantly.

codeExamples.label: Informative image with descriptive alt text
Image with no text alternative

axeRules.description: Ensure every ARIA meter node has an accessible name
Ensure every ARIA progressbar node has an accessible name
Ensure elements have alternative text or a role of none or presentation
Ensure <input type="image"> elements have alternative text
Ensure <object> elements have alternative text
Ensure [role="img"] elements have alternative text
Ensure <svg> elements with an img, graphics-document or graphics-symbol role have accessible text

commonMistakes: Leaving the alt attribute off an entirely, so screen readers announce the file name instead.
Using a <div onClick> or an icon <span> as a button with no aria-label, so it's announced as nothing or as 'group'.
Writing redundant alt text like alt="image of a chart" instead of describing what the chart actually shows.
Giving decorative images descriptive alt text instead of alt="", forcing screen reader users to hear clutter.
Embedding meaningful text in an SVG or icon font with no accessible name (role="img" plus aria-label).

criteria.title: Audio-only and Video-only (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: For prerecorded audio-only and prerecorded video-only media, the following are true, except when the audio or video is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such:Prerecorded Audio-only: An alternative for time-based media is provided that presents equivalent information for prerecorded audio-only content.Prerecorded Video-only: Either an alternative for time-based media or an audio track is provided that presents equivalent information for prerecorded video-only content.
criteria.plainEnglish: Pre-recorded audio-only media (like a podcast) needs a text transcript that carries the same information. Pre-recorded video with no audio (like a silent screen recording) needs either a transcript or an audio track describing what happens. The point is that people who can't hear the audio, or can't see the video, can still get the content another way.
criteria.whyItMatters: Deaf users can't access a podcast that has no transcript, and blind users can't follow a silent how-to video that has no audio description or text alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm every <audio> podcast links to a transcript and every silent <video> has a transcript or descriptive audio track—there's no automated tool for this, so verify it manually.

codeExamples.label: Audio with a linked transcript
Audio with no text alternative
codeExamples.code: <audio controls src="/podcast-ep12.mp3" />

axeRules.description: Ensure <audio> elements have captions

commonMistakes: Publishing a podcast or recorded interview with no transcript at all.
Assuming auto-generated captions count as a transcript for audio-only content—a transcript must be a separate, readable document.
Posting a silent product demo without any text or audio describing what's shown on screen.
Linking a transcript that omits speaker names or non-speech audio that carries meaning.

criteria.title: Captions (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.
criteria.plainEnglish: Any pre-recorded video with sound needs synchronized captions—text that appears in time with the speech and important sounds. Captions let people who are deaf or hard of hearing follow dialogue and know about meaningful audio like a doorbell or laughter. Use a real caption track (a <track kind="captions"> WebVTT file), not just a transcript.
criteria.whyItMatters: About 1 in 5 people have some hearing loss, and without captions they miss the dialogue and meaningful sounds in your video.
criteria.quickCheck: Play the video and confirm captions appear in sync; in markup, check that each <video> has a <track kind="captions"> element—axe's video-caption rule flags missing ones.

codeExamples.label: Video with a synchronized captions track
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/intro.mp4">
  <track
    kind="captions"
    src="/intro.en.vtt"
    srcLang="en"
    label="English"
    default
  />
</video>

codeExamples.label: Video with no captions track
codeExamples.code: <video controls src="/intro.mp4" />

axeRules.description: Ensure <video> elements have captions

commonMistakes: Shipping a <video> with no <track kind="captions"> element.
Providing only a transcript and assuming it satisfies the captions requirement—captions must be synchronized.
Using kind="subtitles" (translation of dialogue) when you mean kind="captions" (which also includes non-speech sounds).
Relying on inaccurate auto-generated captions that are never reviewed.

criteria.title: Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: An alternative for time-based media or audio description of the prerecorded video content is provided for synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.
criteria.plainEnglish: For pre-recorded video with audio, give people who can't see the screen a way to get the visual information: either an audio description track that narrates important visual details during pauses, or a full text alternative (a document describing both audio and visuals). This covers things shown on screen but never spoken aloud.
criteria.whyItMatters: Blind and low-vision users miss on-screen information—like text, charts, or actions that aren't spoken—when a video has no audio description or text alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: Watch the video with your eyes closed: if you lose track of important visual information, it needs an audio description track or a full text alternative.

codeExamples.label: Video with both captions and an audio-description track
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/tutorial.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="/tutorial.en.vtt" srcLang="en" label="English" default />
  <track kind="descriptions" src="/tutorial.desc.vtt" srcLang="en" label="Audio description" />
</video>

codeExamples.label: Video with captions only, no description of visuals
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/tutorial.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="/tutorial.en.vtt" srcLang="en" label="English" default />
</video>

commonMistakes: Assuming captions are enough—captions help deaf users, but blind users need the visuals described.
Skipping audio description because 'the narrator explains everything,' without verifying on-screen text and actions are covered.
Providing a transcript of only the dialogue, omitting any description of the visual content.

criteria.title: Captions (Live)
criteria.officialText: Captions are provided for all live audio content in synchronized media.
criteria.plainEnglish: Live audio in video—webinars, live streams, broadcasts—needs real-time captions as it happens. People who are deaf or hard of hearing need to follow live content too, so caption it using a live captioning service (CART) or your streaming platform's real-time caption feature.
criteria.whyItMatters: Deaf and hard-of-hearing viewers can't follow a live webinar or stream that has no real-time captions.
criteria.quickCheck: Join your own live stream as a viewer and confirm captions appear in near-real-time; there's no automated check for live captioning.

codeExamples.label: Live player wired to a real-time caption track
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/live-stream.m3u8">
  <track
    kind="captions"
    src="/live-captions.vtt"
    srcLang="en"
    label="Live captions"
    default
  />
</video>

codeExamples.label: Live stream with no captions
codeExamples.code: <video controls src="/live-stream.m3u8" />

commonMistakes: Planning to publish captions only after the event instead of captioning it live.
Relying solely on a platform's automatic captions, with no human captioner, for technical or heavily-named content.
Forgetting to turn on the streaming platform's caption feature before going live.

criteria.title: Audio Description (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: Audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AA, pre-recorded video with audio must include an audio description track—narration during natural pauses that describes important visual details. Unlike 1.2.3 (which also allows a text-only alternative), this criterion specifically requires audio description, so blind users get the visual information through sound.
criteria.whyItMatters: Blind users miss critical on-screen action and text when a video lacks an audio description track narrating the visuals.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm the video offers an audio-described version or a <track kind="descriptions">, and verify it by listening with the screen off.

codeExamples.label: Video offering an audio-description track
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/feature.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="/feature.en.vtt" srcLang="en" label="English" default />
  <track kind="descriptions" src="/feature.desc.vtt" srcLang="en" label="Audio description" />
</video>

codeExamples.label: Video with no audio description
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/feature.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="/feature.en.vtt" srcLang="en" label="English" default />
</video>

commonMistakes: Treating audio description as optional because captions already exist.
Adding descriptions that talk over the dialogue instead of fitting them into pauses.
Providing only a text transcript, which doesn't satisfy 1.2.5's specific requirement for audio description.

criteria.title: Sign Language (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: Sign language interpretation is provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, pre-recorded video with audio includes a sign-language interpretation of the audio. Many people who are deaf from birth use a sign language as their first language and read written text more slowly, so a signed interpretation (often shown picture-in-picture) gives them fuller access than captions alone.
criteria.whyItMatters: Deaf users whose first language is a sign language understand a signed interpretation far better than written captions, especially for complex content.
criteria.quickCheck: Check whether the video provides a sign-language interpreter track or a picture-in-picture signed version.

codeExamples.label: Main video with a synchronized sign-language video
Video with no sign-language interpretation
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/lecture.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="/lecture.en.vtt" srcLang="en" label="English" default />
</video>

commonMistakes: Assuming captions remove the need for sign-language interpretation.
Showing the interpreter window so small or low-contrast that the signs are hard to read.
Cropping the interpreter so hands or facial expressions—which carry grammar—are cut off.

criteria.title: Extended Audio Description (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: Where pauses in foreground audio are insufficient to allow audio descriptions to convey the sense of the video, extended audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
criteria.plainEnglish: Sometimes a video's natural pauses are too short to describe everything visual. At Level AAA, you provide extended audio description: the video pauses to let the narration catch up, then resumes. This guarantees blind users get all the visual detail even in fast-paced, densely-narrated content.
criteria.whyItMatters: Blind users miss visual detail in densely-narrated videos where there aren't enough pauses for standard audio description to fit.
criteria.quickCheck: Check whether an extended-audio-description version is offered for any video whose pauses are too brief for full standard description.

codeExamples.label: Video offering an extended audio-description track
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/demo.mp4">
  <track
    kind="descriptions"
    src="/demo.extended-desc.vtt"
    srcLang="en"
    label="Extended audio description"
  />
</video>

codeExamples.label: Video with no extended description for dense visuals
codeExamples.code: <video controls src="/demo.mp4" />

commonMistakes: Cramming descriptions into pauses that are too short, so the narration gets cut off.
Not offering an extended-description version for content-dense video.
Assuming standard audio description (1.2.5) is enough when the visuals are too dense for the available pauses.

criteria.title: Media Alternative (Prerecorded)
criteria.officialText: An alternative for time-based media is provided for all prerecorded synchronized media and for all prerecorded video-only media.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, every pre-recorded video-with-audio (and every silent video) provides a full text alternative—a document describing all the dialogue, sounds, and visual action in order. This serves people who are both deaf and blind (reading with a braille display) as well as anyone who prefers reading.
criteria.whyItMatters: Deafblind users who rely on a braille display can only access your video through a complete text alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm a full text alternative document—dialogue plus visual description—is linked near the video.

codeExamples.label: Video with a linked full text alternative
Video with no full text alternative
codeExamples.code:
<video controls src="/keynote.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="/keynote.en.vtt" srcLang="en" label="English" default />
</video>

commonMistakes: Providing captions and audio description but no single readable text document.
Writing a transcript that lists dialogue but omits the visual scene descriptions.
Putting the transcript behind a download instead of a readable, linkable page.

criteria.title: Audio-only (Live)
criteria.officialText: An alternative for time-based media that presents equivalent information for live audio-only content is provided.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, live audio-only broadcasts (like a live radio stream) provide a text alternative such as real-time captions or a live text feed. This gives deaf and hard-of-hearing listeners equivalent access to live audio that has no accompanying video.
criteria.whyItMatters: Deaf and hard-of-hearing users can't follow a live audio-only broadcast unless it has an accompanying real-time text feed.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm your live audio stream has an accompanying real-time text transcript feed that updates as people speak.

codeExamples.label: Live audio with a real-time text feed
Live audio with no real-time text
codeExamples.code: <audio controls src="/live-radio.m3u8" />

commonMistakes: Offering a transcript only after the broadcast has ended.
Assuming an audio-only live stream is exempt from providing a text alternative.
Streaming live captions into a region that isn't an aria-live area, so screen readers never announce updates.

criteria.title: Info and Relationships
criteria.officialText: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined or are available in text.
criteria.plainEnglish: Visual structure—headings, lists, tables, form labels, grouped fields—must be expressed in the markup, not just with styling. If something looks like a heading, use an <h2>; if it's a list, use <ul>/<li>; associate every form input with a <label>. That way screen readers can convey the same structure that sighted users see.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users navigate by headings, lists, and labels; when structure is faked with CSS instead of semantic HTML, they can't understand relationships or jump around the page.
criteria.quickCheck: Open the Chrome DevTools Accessibility tree (or run axe DevTools) and confirm headings, lists, tables, and form labels are real semantic elements, not styled <div>s.

codeExamples.label: Input with a properly associated label
codeExamples.code:
<label htmlFor="email">Email address</label>
<input id="email" type="email" name="email" />

codeExamples.label: Placeholder used as the only label
codeExamples.code: <input type="email" name="email" placeholder="Email address" />

axeRules.description: Ensure aria-hidden="true" is not present on the document body.
Ensure elements with an ARIA role that require child roles contain them
Ensure elements with an ARIA role that require parent roles are contained by them
Ensure <dl> elements are structured correctly
Ensure <dt> and <dd> elements are contained by a <dl>
Ensure that lists are structured correctly
Ensure <li> elements are used semantically

Ensure bold, italic text and font-size is not used to style

elements as a heading
Ensure that tables with a caption use the <caption> element.
Ensure that each non-empty data cell in a <table> larger than 3 by 3  has one or more table headers
Ensure that each cell in a table that uses the headers attribute refers only to other <th> elements in that table
Ensure that <th> elements and elements with role=columnheader/rowheader have data cells they describe

commonMistakes:
Styling a
or

with big, bold text to look like a heading instead of using

–

.
Building a list out of <div>s and line breaks instead of <ul>/<ol> and <li>.
Using a placeholder as the only label for an input instead of an associated <label>.
Laying out tabular data with <div>s, or a <table> with no <th> headers, so cell relationships are lost.
Grouping radio buttons or checkboxes with no <fieldset> and <legend>.

criteria.title: Meaningful Sequence
criteria.officialText: When the sequence in which content is presented affects its meaning, a correct reading sequence can be programmatically determined.
criteria.plainEnglish: When the order of content matters for understanding, the DOM order must match the reading order, because screen readers and keyboard users follow the source order, not the visual layout. Don't use CSS (flex/grid order, absolute positioning, floats) to visually reorder content in a way that makes the underlying sequence confusing.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users hear content in DOM order, so CSS that visually reorders columns or steps can make them read instructions or data in a nonsensical sequence.
criteria.quickCheck: Disable CSS (or just Tab through the page) and confirm the content still reads in a logical order; watch for flexbox/grid order properties that diverge from the DOM.

codeExamples.label: DOM order matches reading order
codeExamples.code:
{/* No reordering via CSS: source order is the reading order */}
  1. Step 1: Create an account
  2. Step 2: Verify your email
  3. Step 3: Sign in

codeExamples.label: CSS order makes the reading order illogical
codeExamples.code:
{/* Reads visually as 1,2,3 but in the DOM as 3,1,2 */}
  1. Step 1: Create an account
  2. Step 2: Verify your email
  3. Step 3: Sign in

commonMistakes: Using the CSS order property or flex-direction: row-reverse to reorder content visually while the DOM order stays illogical.
Absolutely positioning content so the visual order differs from the source order.
Placing a sidebar before the main content in the DOM just because it appears first visually.

criteria.title: Sensory Characteristics
criteria.officialText: Instructions provided for understanding and operating content do not rely solely on sensory characteristics of components such as shape, color, size, visual location, orientation, or sound.Note: For requirements related to color, refer to Guideline 1.4.
criteria.plainEnglish: Instructions must not rely only on shape, size, color, position, or sound. Saying 'click the round green button on the right' fails people who can't perceive those cues; instead refer to the control by its text label too, like 'click the Submit button.' Combine any sensory cue with a name or text reference.
criteria.whyItMatters: Blind users and people with low vision can't act on instructions like 'press the button on the right' or 'click the green icon' that rely only on shape, position, or color.
criteria.quickCheck: Scan instructional text for words like 'above', 'on the right', 'round', or 'green' used alone—each should also name the element (e.g., its label) so it's identifiable without seeing it.

codeExamples.label: Instruction references the control by its label
codeExamples.code: To save your work, select the Save button at the bottom of the form.

codeExamples.label: Instruction relies only on color and position
codeExamples.code: To save your work, click the green button on the right.

commonMistakes: Writing 'click the button below' or 'see the box on the right' with no other identifier.
Referring to controls only by color, like 'the items marked in red are required.'
Using only a shape or icon ('tap the play icon') with no accompanying accessible name or text.

criteria.title: Orientation
criteria.officialText: Content does not restrict its view and operation to a single display orientation, such as portrait or landscape, unless a specific display orientation is essential.Note: Examples where a particular display orientation may be essential are a bank check, a piano application, slides for a projector or television, or virtual reality content where content is not necessarily restricted to landscape or portrait display orientation.
criteria.plainEnglish: Don't lock your content to only portrait or only landscape. Users with a device mounted in a fixed position (for example, on a wheelchair) may not be able to rotate it, so your layout must work in both orientations—unless a specific orientation is genuinely essential, like a piano app.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users who mount a phone or tablet in a fixed orientation—common for people with motor disabilities—can't use an app that forces a single orientation.
criteria.quickCheck: Rotate your device, or toggle orientation in Chrome DevTools' device toolbar, and confirm the content still works in both portrait and landscape; axe's css-orientation-lock rule flags locking.

codeExamples.label: Layout adapts to any orientation
Content locked to landscape

axeRules.description: Ensure content is not locked to any specific display orientation, and the content is operable in all display orientations

commonMistakes: Using a CSS transform or an @media (orientation) rule to lock or hide content in one orientation.
Setting a native orientation lock that carries into a web view.
Showing a 'please rotate your device' wall instead of supporting both orientations.

criteria.title: Identify Input Purpose
criteria.officialText: The purpose of each input field collecting information about the user can be programmatically determined when:The input field serves a purpose identified in the Input Purposes for user interface components section; andThe content is implemented using technologies with support for identifying the expected meaning for form input data.
criteria.plainEnglish: Form fields that collect known information about the user (name, email, phone, address) should declare their purpose with the HTML autocomplete attribute. This lets browsers autofill correctly and lets assistive tech add familiar icons or personalize fields, reducing effort for users with cognitive or motor disabilities.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities or motor impairments rely on browser autofill and personalized field icons, which only work when inputs declare a valid autocomplete value.
criteria.quickCheck: Inspect personal-data inputs and confirm each has a correct autocomplete value (e.g., autocomplete="email"); axe's autocomplete-valid rule flags invalid tokens.

codeExamples.label: Phone field declaring its purpose
codeExamples.code:
<label htmlFor="tel">Phone</label>
<input id="tel" name="tel" type="tel" autoComplete="tel" />

codeExamples.label: Personal-data field with no autocomplete
codeExamples.code:
<label htmlFor="tel">Phone</label>
<input id="tel" name="tel" type="tel" />

axeRules.description: Ensure the autocomplete attribute is correct and suitable for the form field

commonMistakes: Omitting the autocomplete attribute on name, email, address, and phone fields.
Using a made-up or wrong autocomplete token, like autocomplete="username" on a field that isn't a username.
Disabling autofill with autocomplete="off" on personal-data fields out of habit.
Reusing one autocomplete value across multiple address lines instead of address-line1 and address-line2.

criteria.title: Identify Purpose
criteria.officialText: In content implemented using markup languages, the purpose of user interface components, icons, and regions can be programmatically determined.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, the purpose of UI components, icons, and page regions should be programmatically identifiable—using semantic landmark regions, ARIA, and (where supported) metadata like autocomplete and rel. This lets assistive tools and personalization extensions adapt the interface—swapping icons, hiding ads—to a user's needs.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities who rely on personalization tools to simplify or re-symbolize interfaces need components and regions to expose their purpose programmatically.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm landmarks (<header>, <nav>, <main>, <aside>), icon buttons, and inputs expose their purpose via semantic elements, ARIA roles, and autocomplete.

codeExamples.label: Regions marked with semantic landmarks
codeExamples.code:
<header>{/* ... */}</header>
<nav aria-label="Primary">{/* ... */}</nav>
<main>{/* ... */}</main>
<aside aria-label="Related links">{/* ... */}</aside>

codeExamples.label: Regions built from generic divs
codeExamples.code:
{/* ... */}
{/* ... */}
{/* ... */}
{/* ... */}

commonMistakes: Using generic <div>s for page regions instead of landmark elements like <nav>, <main>, and <aside>.
Icon-only buttons with no role or accessible name describing their purpose.
Omitting autocomplete and rel metadata that lets tools identify a component's purpose.

criteria.title: Use of Color
criteria.officialText: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.Note: This success criterion addresses color perception specifically. Other forms of perception are covered in Guideline 1.3 including programmatic access to color and other visual presentation coding.
criteria.plainEnglish: Color can't be the only way you communicate something. If links are set apart from text only by color, required fields are marked only in red, or a chart's series differ only by hue, then colorblind users and people in high-contrast modes miss the message. Add a second cue: an underline, an icon, text, or a pattern.
criteria.whyItMatters: Roughly 1 in 12 men is colorblind and can't tell, for example, a red 'error' field from a normal one when color is the only signal.
criteria.quickCheck: View the page in grayscale (Chrome DevTools > Rendering > Emulate vision deficiencies > Achromatopsia) and confirm links, states, and meanings are still distinguishable.

codeExamples.label: Error conveyed with an icon and text, not just color
codeExamples.code: ⚠ Error: enter a valid email address.

codeExamples.label: Error conveyed by color alone
codeExamples.code: Enter a valid email address.

axeRules.description: Ensure links are distinguished from surrounding text in a way that does not rely on color

commonMistakes: Removing link underlines so links differ from body text by color alone (axe's link-in-text-block rule flags this).
Marking required or invalid form fields only with red, with no icon or text.
Charts and graphs whose data series are separable only by color, with no labels or patterns.
Showing status (online/offline, pass/fail) with a colored dot and no accompanying text.

criteria.title: Audio Control
criteria.officialText: If any audio on a web page plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, either a mechanism is available to pause or stop the audio, or a mechanism is available to control audio volume independently from the overall system volume level.Note: Since any content that does not meet this success criterion can interfere with a user's ability to use the whole page, all content on the web page (whether or not it is used to meet other success criteria) must meet this success criterion. See Conformance Requirement 5: Non-Interference.
criteria.plainEnglish: If audio plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, give users a way to pause, stop, or mute it independently of their system volume. Auto-playing sound interferes with screen readers (which are also speech) and distracts everyone. The safest practice is not to autoplay audio at all.
criteria.whyItMatters: Auto-playing audio drowns out a screen reader's speech, making the whole page unusable for blind users until they can stop it.
criteria.quickCheck: Load the page and listen: if anything plays for over 3 seconds, confirm there's a visible pause/stop/mute control; axe's no-autoplay-audio rule flags autoplaying media.

codeExamples.label: Media that doesn't autoplay audio
codeExamples.code: <video src="/promo.mp4" controls muted />

codeExamples.label: Autoplaying looping video with sound and no control
codeExamples.code: <video src="/promo.mp4" autoPlay loop />

axeRules.description: Ensure <video> or <audio> elements do not autoplay audio for more than 3 seconds without a control mechanism to stop or mute the audio

commonMistakes: Adding autoplay to a <video> or <audio> with background sound and no stop or mute control.
Relying on the browser to mute autoplay, which isn't guaranteed across browsers.
Hiding or delaying the pause control so users can't stop the audio quickly.

criteria.title: Contrast (Minimum)
criteria.officialText: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for the following:Large Text: Large-scale text and images of large-scale text have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1;Incidental: Text or images of text that are part of an inactive user interface component, that are pure decoration, that are not visible to anyone, or that are part of a picture that contains significant other visual content, have no contrast requirement.Logotypes: Text that is part of a logo or brand name has no contrast requirement.
criteria.plainEnglish: Text must have enough contrast against its background: at least 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text (about 24px, or 19px bold). Low-contrast gray-on-white or light-on-light text is hard to read for people with low vision, aging eyes, or in bright sunlight. Logos and disabled controls are exempt.
criteria.whyItMatters: People with low vision, and the many users over 40 with reduced contrast sensitivity, can't read text whose contrast ratio falls below 4.5:1.
criteria.quickCheck: Use Chrome DevTools' color picker (it shows the contrast ratio with AA/AAA checkmarks) or run axe DevTools' color-contrast rule on the text in question.

codeExamples.label: Body text with sufficient contrast
Body text below the 4.5:1 minimum

axeRules.description: Ensure the contrast between foreground and background colors meets WCAG 2 AA minimum contrast ratio thresholds

commonMistakes: Light-gray placeholder or helper text (e.g., #999 on white) that falls below 4.5:1.
White text on a light brand color or over a busy image with no backing or overlay.
Assuming large headings are fine when they're actually below the 3:1 large-text threshold.
Hover or visited link colors that drop below the contrast minimum.

criteria.title: Resize Text
criteria.officialText: Except for captions and images of text, text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality.
criteria.plainEnglish: Users must be able to zoom text up to 200% without losing content or breaking functionality. Don't disable zoom, and avoid fixed pixel heights or absolute units that cause text to overlap or get clipped when enlarged. Use relative units (rem/em) and flexible containers.
criteria.whyItMatters: Low-vision users who enlarge text to 200% lose content or functionality when layouts are built with fixed sizes or zooming is disabled.
criteria.quickCheck: Zoom the browser to 200% (Cmd/Ctrl +) and confirm no text is clipped or overlapping; check that <meta name="viewport"> doesn't set user-scalable=no or maximum-scale (axe's meta-viewport rule).

codeExamples.label: Viewport that allows zooming
codeExamples.code: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />

codeExamples.label: Viewport that disables zooming
codeExamples.code: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=no" />

axeRules.description: Ensure <meta name="viewport"> does not disable text scaling and zooming

commonMistakes: Setting user-scalable=no or maximum-scale=1 in the viewport meta tag, disabling zoom.
Fixed-height containers with overflow: hidden that clip text when it grows.
Sizing text and containers in px so enlarged text overflows or overlaps.
Using viewport units (vw) for font size, which don't respond to the user's zoom or font settings.

criteria.title: Images of Text
criteria.officialText: If the technologies being used can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text except for the following:Customizable: The image of text can be visually customized to the user's requirements;Essential: A particular presentation of text is essential to the information being conveyed.Note: Logotypes (text that is part of a logo or brand name) are considered essential.
criteria.plainEnglish: Use real text, not pictures of text, whenever your technology can produce the same visual result with CSS. Text baked into an image can't be resized crisply, recolored, translated, or read by a screen reader. Logos and cases where an exact presentation is essential are exempt.
criteria.whyItMatters: Low-vision users who enlarge or recolor text, and screen reader users, can't access words that are baked into an image instead of being real text.
criteria.quickCheck: Look for headings, buttons, or banners that are images and try to select their text—if you can't, it's an image of text that should be real text styled with CSS.

codeExamples.label: Heading rendered as real, styled text
codeExamples.code: Summer sale — 50% off everything

codeExamples.label: Heading delivered as an image of text

commonMistakes: Exporting headings or marketing banners as PNG/JPG instead of styling real text with a web font.
Putting button or navigation labels inside background images.
Using an image of text for pricing tables or feature lists that change often.

criteria.title: Contrast (Enhanced)
criteria.officialText: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 7:1, except for the following:Large Text: Large-scale text and images of large-scale text have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1;Incidental: Text or images of text that are part of an inactive user interface component, that are pure decoration, that are not visible to anyone, or that are part of a picture that contains significant other visual content, have no contrast requirement.Logotypes: Text that is part of a logo or brand name has no contrast requirement.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the stricter Level AAA version of contrast: normal text needs at least 7:1 and large text at least 4.5:1 against its background. It's aimed at giving people with moderately low vision (around 20/80) readable text without assistive technology. The same exemptions as 1.4.3 apply (logos, disabled controls).
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with moderately low vision (around 20/80) need a 7:1 contrast ratio to read normal-size text comfortably without assistive technology.
criteria.quickCheck: Use Chrome DevTools' color picker and confirm the contrast meets the AAA threshold (7:1 for normal text, 4.5:1 for large); axe's color-contrast-enhanced rule checks AAA.

codeExamples.label: Body text meeting the AAA 7:1 threshold
Body text that passes AA but fails AAA

axeRules.description: Ensure the contrast between foreground and background colors meets WCAG 2 AAA enhanced contrast ratio thresholds

commonMistakes: Designing only to the AA 4.5:1 minimum when AAA conformance requires 7:1.
Mid-gray body text that passes AA but falls short of 7:1.
Brand colors used for text that can't reach 7:1 without adjustment.

criteria.title: Low or No Background Audio
criteria.officialText: For prerecorded audio-only content that (1) contains primarily speech in the foreground, (2) is not an audio CAPTCHA or audio logo, and (3) is not vocalization intended to be primarily musical expression such as singing or rapping, at least one of the following is true:No Background: The audio does not contain background sounds.Turn Off: The background sounds can be turned off.20 dB: The background sounds are at least 20 decibels lower than the foreground speech content, with the exception of occasional sounds that last for only one or two seconds.Note: Per the definition of "decibel," background sound that meets this requirement will be approximately four times quieter than the foreground speech content.
criteria.plainEnglish: For pre-recorded speech audio, background sounds should be absent, able to be switched off, or at least 20 dB quieter than the speech (about four times quieter). This helps people who are hard of hearing separate the foreground voice from music or noise.
criteria.whyItMatters: Hard-of-hearing users struggle to separate speech from background music or noise unless the background is significantly quieter or can be turned off.
criteria.quickCheck: Listen to speech recordings and confirm any background sound is clearly quieter than the voice, or that a version with the background removed is offered.

codeExamples.label: Speech mixed well above a quiet background
codeExamples.code:
{/* Foreground speech mixed at least 20 dB above any background bed */}
<audio controls>
  <source src="/interview-clean-voice.mp3" type="audio/mpeg" />
</audio>

codeExamples.label: Speech buried under a loud music bed
codeExamples.code: <audio controls src="/interview-loud-music-bed.mp3" />

commonMistakes: Mixing background music at nearly the same level as the narration.
Offering no version of the audio with the background music removed or lowered.
Keeping continuous background sound under speech instead of limiting it to brief, occasional moments.

criteria.title: Visual Presentation
criteria.officialText: For the visual presentation of blocks of text, a mechanism is available to achieve the following:Foreground and background colors can be selected by the user.Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK).Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins).Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing.Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent in a way that does not require the user to scroll horizontally to read a line of text on a full-screen window.Note 1: Content is not required to use these values. The requirement is that a mechanism is available for users to change these presentation aspects. The mechanism can be provided by the browser or other user agent. Content is not required to provide the mechanism.Note 2: Writing systems for some languages use different presentation aspects to improve readability and legibility. If a presentation aspect in this success criterion is not used in a writing system, content in that writing system does not need to use that presentation setting and can conform without it. Authors are encouraged to follow guidance for improving readability and legibility of text in their writing system.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, blocks of text must let users control presentation for readability: selectable foreground/background colors, lines no wider than 80 characters, no full justification, line spacing of at least 1.5 within paragraphs (with larger gaps between paragraphs), and resizing to 200% without horizontal scrolling. Provide these defaults or a mechanism to set them.
criteria.whyItMatters: People with dyslexia and low vision lose their place in text that is fully justified, too wide, or tightly spaced, and need control over these presentation settings.
criteria.quickCheck: Check body-text blocks: max width around 80 characters, line-height at least 1.5, not justified, and readable at 200% zoom without horizontal scrolling.

codeExamples.label: Readable text block: left-aligned, ~80 chars, 1.5 line-height
Justified, full-width, tightly-spaced text

commonMistakes: Using text-align: justify on body copy, creating uneven 'rivers' of whitespace.
Full-width lines that far exceed 80 characters because there's no max-width.
Cramped line-height below 1.5 in long-form content.

criteria.title: Images of Text (No Exception)
criteria.officialText: Images of text are only used for pure decoration or where a particular presentation of text is essential to the information being conveyed.Note: Logotypes (text that is part of a logo or brand name) are considered essential.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the Level AAA version of 1.4.5, with no 'customizable' exception: images of text are only allowed for pure decoration or when a specific presentation is truly essential (logos count as essential). In practice, render all meaningful text as real text.
criteria.whyItMatters: Low-vision and screen reader users can't resize, recolor, or read any meaningful text that's delivered as an image rather than as real text.
criteria.quickCheck: Try to select the text in any image-like element; meaningful text that can't be selected should be real text, with images reserved for logos and decoration.

codeExamples.label: Quote rendered as real, styled text
codeExamples.code: Design is how it works.

codeExamples.label: Quote delivered as an image of text

commonMistakes: Using images of text for headings or pull-quotes to get a specific font, instead of loading that font as a web font.
Image-based buttons or navigation labels.
Rasterizing styled text into an image because a designer handed off a PNG, rather than reproducing it with CSS.

criteria.title: Reflow
criteria.officialText: Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for:Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels;Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels.Except for parts of the content which require two-dimensional layout for usage or meaning.Note 1: 320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom. For web content which is designed to scroll horizontally (e.g., with vertical text), 256 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport height of 1024 CSS pixels at 400% zoom.Note 2: Examples of content which requires two-dimensional layout are images required for understanding (such as maps and diagrams), video, games, presentations, data tables (not individual cells), and interfaces where it is necessary to keep toolbars in view while manipulating content. It is acceptable to provide two-dimensional scrolling for such parts of the content.
criteria.plainEnglish: Content must reflow into a single column without requiring two-dimensional scrolling at a width of 320 CSS pixels (equivalent to 400% zoom on a 1280px screen). Users shouldn't have to scroll both horizontally and vertically to read. Use responsive, flexible layouts; only things like data tables or maps may scroll in two dimensions.
criteria.whyItMatters: Low-vision users who zoom to 400% are forced to scroll back and forth on every line when content doesn't reflow to a single column.
criteria.quickCheck: Set the viewport to 320px wide (Chrome DevTools device toolbar) or zoom to 400% and confirm no horizontal scrolling is needed to read the content.

codeExamples.label: Layout that reflows to one column
Fixed-width layout that forces horizontal scroll

commonMistakes: Fixed-width (px) containers that don't shrink below the viewport, causing horizontal scroll.
Multi-column layouts that don't collapse to a single column on narrow viewports.
A min-width on the page, or large fixed-position elements, that overflow at 320px.
Using white-space: nowrap on long content so it can't wrap.

criteria.title: Non-text Contrast
criteria.officialText: The visual presentation of the following have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent color(s):User Interface Components: Visual information required to identify user interface components and states, except for inactive components or where the appearance of the component is determined by the user agent and not modified by the author;Graphical Objects: Parts of graphics required to understand the content, except when a particular presentation of graphics is essential to the information being conveyed.
criteria.plainEnglish: Important non-text visuals need at least 3:1 contrast against what's next to them. That includes UI component boundaries and states—a button's edge, an input's border, focus indicators, toggle states—and the meaningful parts of icons and graphics. Without it, users with low vision can't tell where controls are or what state they're in.
criteria.whyItMatters: Low-vision users can't locate buttons, form fields, or focus indicators when their borders and states have less than 3:1 contrast with the surroundings.
criteria.quickCheck: Run a contrast tool on control borders, focus outlines, and meaningful icon shapes against their adjacent colors and confirm each is at least 3:1.

codeExamples.label: Input border with sufficient contrast
Input border too faint to perceive

commonMistakes: Input fields whose only border is a faint light-gray line below 3:1 against the background.
Custom focus outlines that are too low-contrast, or removed entirely.
Toggle/switch components where on vs. off is shown only by a subtle low-contrast color shift.
Icon-only buttons whose glyph doesn't reach 3:1 against the button background.

criteria.title: Text Spacing
criteria.officialText: In content implemented using markup languages that support the following text style properties, no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property:Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size;Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size;Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size;Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size.Exception: Human languages and scripts that do not make use of one or more of these text style properties in written text can conform using only the properties that exist for that combination of language and script.Note 1: Content is not required to use these text spacing values. The requirement is to ensure that when a user overrides the authored text spacing, content or functionality is not lost.Note 2: Writing systems for some languages use different text spacing settings, such as paragraph start indent. Authors are encouraged to follow locally available guidance for improving readability and legibility of text in their writing system.
criteria.plainEnglish: Users must be able to override text spacing—line height to 1.5x, paragraph spacing to 2x, letter spacing to 0.12x, and word spacing to 0.16x the font size—without losing content or breaking the layout. Don't trap text in tight containers that clip or overlap when a user's stylesheet bumps the spacing up.
criteria.whyItMatters: People with dyslexia and low vision who increase text spacing to read more easily lose content when it gets clipped or overlaps in rigid containers.
criteria.quickCheck: Apply a text-spacing bookmarklet (or set line-height:1.5; letter-spacing:.12em; word-spacing:.16em via DevTools) and confirm nothing clips or overlaps; axe's avoid-inline-spacing flags inline !important spacing.

codeExamples.label: Container that grows with increased spacing
Fixed-height container that clips spaced text

axeRules.description: Ensure that text spacing set through style attributes can be adjusted with custom stylesheets

commonMistakes: Fixed-height buttons or containers with overflow: hidden that clip text when spacing increases.
Setting line-height or letter-spacing inline with !important, which blocks user overrides (axe avoid-inline-spacing).
Truncating text with fixed dimensions that can't grow to accommodate more spacing.

criteria.title: Content on Hover or Focus
criteria.officialText: Where receiving and then removing pointer hover or keyboard focus triggers additional content to become visible and then hidden, the following are true:Dismissible: A mechanism is available to dismiss the additional content without moving pointer hover or keyboard focus, unless the additional content communicates an input error or does not obscure or replace other content;Hoverable: If pointer hover can trigger the additional content, then the pointer can be moved over the additional content without the additional content disappearing;Persistent: The additional content remains visible until the hover or focus trigger is removed, the user dismisses it, or its information is no longer valid.Exception: The visual presentation of the additional content is controlled by the user agent and is not modified by the author.Note 1: Examples of additional content controlled by the user agent include browser tooltips created through use of the HTML title attribute [HTML].Note 2: Custom tooltips, sub-menus, and other nonmodal popups that display on hover and focus are examples of additional content covered by this criterion.Note 3: This criterion applies to content that appears in addition to the triggering component itself. Since hidden components that are made visible on keyboard focus (such as links used to skip to another part of a page) do not present additional content they are not covered by this criterion.
criteria.plainEnglish: Extra content that appears on hover or focus—tooltips, popovers, custom dropdowns—must be dismissible (you can close it, e.g., with Escape, without moving the pointer or focus), hoverable (you can move the mouse onto it without it vanishing), and persistent (it stays until you dismiss it or move away). This helps people who zoom, use a keyboard, or have hand tremors.
criteria.whyItMatters: Low-vision users who zoom in and keyboard users lose access to tooltips and popovers that vanish the moment the pointer moves or that can't be dismissed.
criteria.quickCheck: Trigger a tooltip or popover, then try to move the mouse onto it (it should stay), press Escape (it should close), and confirm it doesn't auto-disappear while you're reading.

codeExamples.label: Tooltip that is hoverable, dismissible, and persistent
title attribute: not hoverable or dismissible
codeExamples.code: <button title="Your username must be 3–20 characters.">Username</button>

commonMistakes: Tooltips that disappear the instant the pointer leaves the trigger, so users can't move onto them.
No way to dismiss a popover with the keyboard (Escape).
Hover content that auto-hides on a timer while the user is still reading it.
Using the native title attribute for important info, which isn't hoverable or dismissible and is invisible to touch and keyboard.

criteria.title: Keyboard
criteria.officialText: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes, except where the underlying function requires input that depends on the path of the user's movement and not just the endpoints.Note 1: This exception relates to the underlying function, not the input technique. For example, if using handwriting to enter text, the input technique (handwriting) requires path-dependent input but the underlying function (text input) does not.Note 2: This does not forbid and should not discourage providing mouse input or other input methods in addition to keyboard operation.
criteria.plainEnglish: Everything a user can do with a mouse must also work with the keyboard alone—activating buttons and links, opening menus, operating custom widgets. Keyboard users tab to elements and press Enter, Space, or arrow keys, so don't build interactions that respond only to mouse events. The one exception is functionality that inherently depends on the path of movement, like freehand drawing.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard-only users—including people with motor disabilities and blind users who rely on screen readers—can't use any control that responds only to a mouse click or hover.
criteria.quickCheck: Put your mouse away and Tab through the page, confirming you can reach and operate every interactive element with Tab, Enter, Space, and the arrow keys.

codeExamples.label: Real button, operable by keyboard
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={handleSave}>Save</button>

codeExamples.label: Clickable div that keyboard users can't reach
codeExamples.code: Save

axeRules.description: Ensure <frame> and <iframe> elements with focusable content do not have tabindex=-1
Ensure elements that have scrollable content are accessible by keyboard in Safari
Ensure that server-side image maps are not used

commonMistakes: Building clickable controls from <div> or <span> with onClick instead of <button>, so they can't be focused or activated by keyboard.
Custom widgets (menus, tabs, sliders) that respond to mouse events but have no keydown handlers for Enter, Space, or arrow keys.
Using onMouseOver/hover to reveal content with no keyboard-focus equivalent.
Scrollable regions with no focusable element, so keyboard users can't scroll them (axe scrollable-region-focusable).
Custom controls with a missing or positive tabindex that drops them out of the natural tab order.

criteria.title: No Keyboard Trap
criteria.officialText: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface, and, if it requires more than unmodified arrow or tab keys or other standard exit methods, the user is advised of the method for moving focus away.Note: Since any content that does not meet this success criterion can interfere with a user's ability to use the whole page, all content on the web page (whether it is used to meet other success criteria or not) must meet this success criterion. See Conformance Requirement 5: Non-Interference.
criteria.plainEnglish: If a keyboard user can Tab into a component, they must be able to Tab back out using standard keys. A 'keyboard trap' is when focus gets stuck—often in custom modals, embedded widgets, or media players—so the user can't move on. If exiting requires a non-standard key, you must tell the user how.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard-only users get completely stuck on the page when focus enters a component—like a modal or embedded widget—that it can't escape from.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through every widget, modal, and embed and confirm you can always Tab (or Shift+Tab) back out without reaching for the mouse.

codeExamples.label: Modal you can escape with the keyboard
Modal that traps keyboard focus

commonMistakes: A custom modal that holds focus but provides no Escape key or keyboard-reachable close button.
Embedded third-party widgets (date pickers, video players) that capture Tab and never release it.
A focus-trap implementation that keeps looping focus inside a component even after it's closed.
Calling preventDefault on the Tab keydown without redirecting focus, swallowing the key.

criteria.title: Keyboard (No Exception)
criteria.officialText: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the Level AAA version of 2.1.1: all functionality must be keyboard operable with no exceptions—even path-dependent actions like freehand drawing need a keyboard-operable alternative. Aim for full keyboard parity across the entire interface.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard-only users are excluded from any feature—including path-based ones like drawing or signature capture—that has no keyboard-operable alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through the whole app and confirm every feature, including drag and draw interactions, has a keyboard-operable path with no mouse-only exceptions.

codeExamples.label: Drag to reorder, with keyboard buttons as an equivalent
codeExamples.code: • Item A

codeExamples.label: Reordering only possible by mouse drag
codeExamples.code: • Item A

axeRules.description: Ensure elements that have scrollable content are accessible by keyboard in Safari

commonMistakes: Freehand drawing or signature tools with no keyboard alternative for entering the same data.
Drag-and-drop reordering that can't be performed with keyboard keys.
Relying on the 2.1.1 path-dependent exception, which does not apply at Level AAA.

criteria.title: Character Key Shortcuts
criteria.officialText: If a keyboard shortcut is implemented in content using only letter (including upper- and lower-case letters), punctuation, number, or symbol characters, then at least one of the following is true:Turn off: A mechanism is available to turn the shortcut off;Remap: A mechanism is available to remap the shortcut to include one or more non-printable keyboard keys (e.g., Ctrl, Alt);Active only on focus: The keyboard shortcut for a user interface component is only active when that component has focus.
criteria.plainEnglish: If you add single-character keyboard shortcuts (like pressing 'j' to jump to the next item), give users a way to turn them off, remap them, or make them active only when the relevant component has focus. Otherwise people using speech input, or who bump keys accidentally, trigger actions they never intended.
criteria.whyItMatters: Speech-input users (whose spoken words emit keystrokes) and people with tremors accidentally trigger single-key shortcuts, firing unintended actions.
criteria.quickCheck: List any single-character shortcuts in your app and confirm each can be disabled, remapped, or only fires when its component has focus.

codeExamples.label: Shortcut requires a modifier and a focused component
Global single-key shortcut with no opt-out

commonMistakes: Adding global single-key shortcuts (e.g., 'r' to reply) with no way to disable or remap them.
Binding shortcuts to document-level keydown so they fire even while the user is typing elsewhere.
Not requiring a modifier (Ctrl/Alt) and not scoping the shortcut to a focused component.

criteria.title: Timing Adjustable
criteria.officialText: For each time limit that is set by the content, at least one of the following is true:Turn off: The user is allowed to turn off the time limit before encountering it; orAdjust: The user is allowed to adjust the time limit before encountering it over a wide range that is at least ten times the length of the default setting; orExtend: The user is warned before time expires and given at least 20 seconds to extend the time limit with a simple action (for example, "press the space bar"), and the user is allowed to extend the time limit at least ten times; orReal-time Exception: The time limit is a required part of a real-time event (for example, an auction), and no alternative to the time limit is possible; orEssential Exception: The time limit is essential and extending it would invalidate the activity; or20 Hour Exception: The time limit is longer than 20 hours.Note: This success criterion helps ensure that users can complete tasks without unexpected changes in content or context that are a result of a time limit. This success criterion should be considered in conjunction with Success Criterion 3.2.1, which puts limits on changes of content or context as a result of user action.
criteria.plainEnglish: If your content imposes a time limit—a session timeout, an expiring form, a countdown—let users turn it off, adjust it, or extend it (with a warning and at least 20 seconds to respond). People who read slowly or use assistive tech need more time. A few real-time exceptions like auctions apply.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities, low vision, or motor impairments who need more time lose their work or get logged out when a time limit can't be extended.
criteria.quickCheck: Identify every time limit (session timeout, redirect, countdown) and confirm users can turn it off, extend it, or are warned with 20+ seconds to act; check for <meta http-equiv="refresh"> (axe meta-refresh).

codeExamples.label: Warn before timeout and let the user extend
Forced timed redirect with no user control
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Redirects after 30s with no way to stop or extend it -->
<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="30; url=/timed-out" />

axeRules.description: Ensure <meta http-equiv="refresh"> is not used for delayed refresh

commonMistakes: Using <meta http-equiv="refresh"> to auto-redirect or reload on a timer (axe meta-refresh).
Session timeouts that log users out with no warning or way to extend.
Forms that silently expire and discard input after a fixed time.
Auto-advancing carousels or quizzes with a countdown that can't be paused or extended.

criteria.title: Pause, Stop, Hide
criteria.officialText: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, all of the following are true:Moving, blinking, scrolling: For any moving, blinking or scrolling information that (1) starts automatically, (2) lasts more than five seconds, and (3) is presented in parallel with other content, there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop, or hide it unless the movement, blinking, or scrolling is part of an activity where it is essential; andAuto-updating: For any auto-updating information that (1) starts automatically and (2) is presented in parallel with other content, there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop, or hide it or to control the frequency of the update unless the auto-updating is part of an activity where it is essential.Note 1: For requirements related to flickering or flashing content, refer to Guideline 2.3.Note 2: Since any content that does not meet this success criterion can interfere with a user's ability to use the whole page, all content on the web page (whether it is used to meet other success criteria or not) must meet this success criterion. See Conformance Requirement 5: Non-Interference.Note 3: Content that is updated periodically by software or that is streamed to the user agent is not required to preserve or present information that is generated or received between the initiation of the pause and resuming presentation, as this may not be technically possible, and in many situations could be misleading to do so.Note 4: An animation that occurs as part of a preload phase or similar situation can be considered essential if interaction cannot occur during that phase for all users and if not indicating progress could confuse users or cause them to think that content was frozen or broken.
criteria.plainEnglish: Any content that moves, blinks, scrolls, or auto-updates—carousels, animations, news tickers, live feeds—that starts automatically, lasts more than 5 seconds, and sits alongside other content must have a control to pause, stop, or hide it. Motion is distracting or disabling for many users, so give them an off switch.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with attention-related, cognitive, or vestibular disabilities can't focus on or read a page when auto-playing motion or auto-updating content can't be paused.
criteria.quickCheck: Look for any auto-starting motion or auto-updating content lasting over 5 seconds and confirm there's a visible pause/stop/hide control; axe flags <blink> and <marquee>.

codeExamples.label: Carousel with a pause control
codeExamples.code: {/* auto-advances only while !paused */}

codeExamples.label: Carousel that auto-advances with no pause

axeRules.description: Ensure <blink> elements are not used
Ensure <marquee> elements are not used

commonMistakes: Auto-rotating carousels or sliders with no pause button.
Using <marquee> or <blink>, or CSS animations that loop indefinitely with no control (axe blink/marquee).
Auto-refreshing feeds or live tickers with no way to pause or slow the updates.
Background video or animation that loops with no stop control.

criteria.title: No Timing
criteria.officialText: Timing is not an essential part of the event or activity presented by the content, except for non-interactive synchronized media and real-time events.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, your content imposes no time limits at all on interactions (except non-interactive media and real-time events like a live webinar). Users can take as long as they need to read content and complete tasks.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users who need significantly more time—due to cognitive, motor, or reading disabilities—are excluded by any non-essential time limit on a task.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm no interactive task in your app imposes a time limit other than live or real-time events.

codeExamples.label: No countdown — the user submits when ready
codeExamples.code:
// No timer: the form is submitted only when the user chooses
<form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>{/* ... */}</form>

codeExamples.label: Quiz that auto-submits on a timer

commonMistakes: Imposing a countdown on quizzes or checkout flows that isn't strictly essential.
Auto-logging out idle users when the activity doesn't truly require it.
Auto-advancing slideshows or steps that move on before the user has finished.

criteria.title: Interruptions
criteria.officialText: Interruptions can be postponed or suppressed by the user, except interruptions involving an emergency.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, users can postpone or suppress interruptions—popups, toast notifications, auto-updates, chat nudges—except genuine emergencies. Unexpected interruptions disrupt people who are concentrating or using assistive technology.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities, and those using screen readers, lose their place and focus when notifications or auto-updates interrupt them unexpectedly.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm users can mute or defer notifications and auto-updates, and check there's no <meta http-equiv="refresh"> (axe meta-refresh-no-exceptions).

codeExamples.label: Respect a user preference before interrupting
Unconditional, unsuppressable interruptions
codeExamples.code:
// Pops up every 10s with no way to suppress
setInterval(() => showToast("New activity!"), 10000);

axeRules.description: Ensure <meta http-equiv="refresh"> is not used for delayed refresh

commonMistakes: Toast notifications or modals that pop up unprompted with no way to mute them.
Using <meta http-equiv="refresh"> to reload content (axe meta-refresh-no-exceptions).
Pushing live updates that steal focus or shift content while the user is reading.

criteria.title: Re-authenticating
criteria.officialText: When an authenticated session expires, the user can continue the activity without loss of data after re-authenticating.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, if an authenticated session expires while a user is working, they can log back in and continue without losing any data. Preserve form input and state across re-authentication so a timeout doesn't destroy someone's work.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users who work slowly due to a disability are the most likely to hit a session timeout and lose everything they entered if data isn't preserved across re-login.
criteria.quickCheck: Let a session expire mid-form, log back in, and confirm your entered data is still there.

codeExamples.label: Persist the draft so it survives re-authentication
Redirect on expiry, losing all in-memory state
codeExamples.code:
// On 401, redirect to login and lose all unsaved form state
if (res.status === 401) window.location.href = "/login";

commonMistakes: Discarding form data when the session expires instead of restoring it after re-login.
Redirecting expired sessions to a login page that loses the user's place and input.
Showing a session-expired error that forces a full restart of a multi-step form.

criteria.title: Timeouts
criteria.officialText: Users are warned of the duration of any user inactivity that could cause data loss, unless the data is preserved for more than 20 hours when the user does not take any actions.Note: Privacy regulations may require explicit user consent before user identification has been authenticated and before user data is preserved. In cases where the user is a minor, explicit consent may not be solicited in most jurisdictions, countries or regions. Consultation with privacy professionals and legal counsel is advised when considering data preservation as an approach to satisfy this success criterion.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, warn users up front how long inactivity can last before they lose data—unless you preserve their data for more than 20 hours. People may step away mid-task, and they need to know whether their work will survive.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users who need to pause a task—for a break or due to a disability—lose unsaved data without warning if they don't know the inactivity limit.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm the app warns users about the inactivity-timeout duration up front, or that data is preserved for 20+ hours.

codeExamples.label: Warn about the inactivity limit up front
codeExamples.code: For your security, you'll be signed out after 15 minutes of inactivity. Unsaved changes will be lost.

codeExamples.label: Silent data loss after idle time
codeExamples.code:
// Clears the draft after idle time with no prior warning
setTimeout(() => clearDraft(), 15 * 60 * 1000);

commonMistakes: Setting an inactivity data-loss timeout with no advance warning of its duration.
Assuming users know how long they can be idle before losing work.
Not preserving in-progress data across the timeout, so idle users lose everything.

criteria.title: Three Flashes or Below Threshold
criteria.officialText: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period, or the flash is below the general flash and red flash thresholds.Note: Since any content that does not meet this success criterion can interfere with a user's ability to use the whole page, all content on the web page (whether it is used to meet other success criteria or not) must meet this success criterion. See Conformance Requirement 5: Non-Interference.
criteria.plainEnglish: Nothing on the page may flash more than three times per second, unless the flash is small and dim enough to stay below the established flash thresholds. Rapid flashing can trigger seizures in people with photosensitive epilepsy.
criteria.whyItMatters: Content flashing more than three times per second can trigger seizures in people with photosensitive epilepsy.
criteria.quickCheck: Review animations, GIFs, and video for anything flashing more than three times per second, and analyze suspect media with a tool like PEAT (the Photosensitive Epilepsy Analysis Tool).

codeExamples.label: Gentle pulse, well under 3 flashes/second
Strobe flashing ~10 times/second

commonMistakes: Auto-playing GIFs or videos with rapid flashing or strobe effects.
CSS or JS animations that rapidly flash large areas more than three times per second.
Flashing alerts or attention-grabbing effects above the flash threshold.

criteria.title: Three Flashes
criteria.officialText: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the stricter Level AAA rule: nothing flashes more than three times in any one second—period, with no 'below threshold' exception based on size or brightness. Avoid all rapid flashing.
criteria.whyItMatters: Even small or dim flashing above three times per second can trigger seizures in highly photosensitive users, who get no exception at this level.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm no content—regardless of how small or dim—flashes more than three times in any one-second period.

codeExamples.label: Smooth animation, no rapid flashing
Rapid flashing regardless of size

commonMistakes: Relying on the 2.3.1 'below threshold' allowance, which does not exist at Level AAA.
Small flashing indicators or animated cursors that exceed three flashes per second.
Spinners or loaders that strobe rapidly rather than animate smoothly.

criteria.title: Animation from Interactions
criteria.officialText: Motion animation triggered by interaction can be disabled, unless the animation is essential to the functionality or the information being conveyed.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, motion animations triggered by user interaction—parallax, large transitions, movement on scroll—can be turned off, unless the animation is essential. Honor the prefers-reduced-motion setting so people with vestibular disorders aren't made dizzy.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with vestibular disorders can feel dizziness or nausea from large motion animations (parallax, scroll effects) that they can't disable.
criteria.quickCheck: Enable 'reduce motion' in your OS and confirm non-essential animations are reduced or removed; check that your CSS honors @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce).

codeExamples.label: Animation disabled when reduced motion is preferred
Large parallax motion with no opt-out

commonMistakes: Parallax scrolling or large transition animations with no prefers-reduced-motion fallback.
Ignoring the @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) media query entirely.
Motion triggered on scroll or hover that can't be turned off.

criteria.title: Bypass Blocks
criteria.officialText: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple web pages.
criteria.plainEnglish: Give users a way to skip past content repeated on every page—like the header and nav—straight to the main content. A 'skip to main content' link or proper landmark regions (<main>, <nav>) lets keyboard and screen reader users avoid tabbing through the same menu on every page.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard and screen reader users otherwise have to tab through dozens of repeated navigation links on every single page before reaching the content.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab once from the top of the page and confirm a 'Skip to main content' link appears, or verify there are <main>/<nav> landmarks (axe's bypass rule).

codeExamples.label: Skip link plus a main landmark
codeExamples.code:
Skip to main content
{/* repeated navigation */}
{/* page content */}

codeExamples.label: No way to bypass the repeated nav
codeExamples.code:
{/* repeated navigation, no skip mechanism */}
{/* page content */}

axeRules.description: Ensure each page has at least one mechanism for a user to bypass navigation and jump straight to the content

commonMistakes: No skip link and no landmark regions, so users must tab through the full nav on every page.
A skip link permanently hidden with display:none instead of visually-hidden-until-focused.
A skip link whose target has no id or isn't focusable, so it doesn't actually move focus.
Wrapping everything in <div>s instead of using <main>, <nav>, and <header> landmarks.

criteria.title: Page Titled
criteria.officialText: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.
criteria.plainEnglish: Every page needs a unique, descriptive <title> that says what the page is about. The title is the first thing a screen reader announces and what shows in browser tabs, history, and bookmarks, so 'Untitled' or a generic site name on every page leaves users lost.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users rely on the page title—announced first on load—to know which page they're on; a missing or generic title leaves them disoriented, especially with many tabs open.
criteria.quickCheck: Check the browser tab text and the <title> element and confirm it's non-empty and describes this specific page (axe's document-title rule).

codeExamples.label: Descriptive, page-specific title
codeExamples.code: <title>Checkout — Step 2 of 3 | Acme Store</title>

codeExamples.label: Generic title repeated on every page
codeExamples.code: <title>Acme Store</title>

axeRules.description: Ensure each HTML document contains a non-empty <title> element

commonMistakes: A missing or empty <title> element (axe document-title).
Using the same generic title (like just the site name) on every page.
In single-page apps, never updating document.title on route changes.
Putting the brand name first so the unique part is cut off in the browser tab.

criteria.title: Focus Order
criteria.officialText: If a web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operability.
criteria.plainEnglish: When users tab through a page, focus must move in an order that makes sense and preserves meaning—generally matching the visual reading order. Don't let focus jump around unpredictably. Avoid positive tabindex values, which override the natural DOM order and cause confusing jumps.
