criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard and screen reader users get confused, and can submit forms incorrectly, when Tab moves focus in an illogical order that doesn't match the visual layout.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through the page and confirm focus moves in a logical order matching the visual flow; search your code for any tabindex value greater than 0.

codeExamples.label: Focus follows the natural DOM and visual order
codeExamples.code:
{/* No tabindex overrides: focus order matches the visual order */}
<input name="first" />
<input name="last" />
<button type="submit">Next</button>

codeExamples.label: Positive tabindex forces a confusing order
codeExamples.code:
{/* Focus jumps 3 → 1 → 2 because of positive tabindex */}
<input name="first" tabIndex={3} />
<input name="last" tabIndex={1} />
<button type="submit" tabIndex={2}>Next</button>

commonMistakes: Using positive tabindex (tabindex="1", "2"...), which hijacks the tab order and causes jumps.
A DOM order that doesn't match the visual order, so focus appears to jump around.
Opening a modal without moving focus into it, leaving focus behind on the page.
Inserting new content (like an expanded panel) without placing focus logically.

criteria.title: Link Purpose (In Context)
criteria.officialText: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or from the link text together with its programmatically determined link context, except where the purpose of the link would be ambiguous to users in general.
criteria.plainEnglish: A link's purpose should be clear from its text, or from its text plus its immediate context (the sentence, list item, or table cell it sits in). Avoid bare 'click here' or 'read more' links that make no sense out of context—screen reader users often pull up a list of all links with no surrounding text.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users who navigate by a list of links hear only the link text; a page full of 'click here' or 'read more' gives them no idea where each link goes.
criteria.quickCheck: Read your link text out of context (e.g., in the browser's or screen reader's links list) and confirm each describes its destination; axe's link-name rule flags empty links.

codeExamples.label: Self-describing link text
codeExamples.code: Download the 2024 annual report (PDF)

codeExamples.label: Link purpose hidden behind 'click here'
codeExamples.code: To get the 2024 annual report, click here.

axeRules.description: Ensure <area> elements of image maps have alternative text
Ensure links have discernible text

commonMistakes: Generic link text like 'click here', 'read more', or 'learn more' repeated across the page.
Icon-only links (a bare arrow or image) with no accessible name (axe link-name).
Image links and image-map <area>s with no alt text (axe area-alt).
Multiple 'Read more' links that are indistinguishable to a screen reader.

criteria.title: Multiple Ways
criteria.officialText: More than one way is available to locate a web page within a set of web pages except where the web page is the result of, or a step in, a process.
criteria.plainEnglish: Within a set of pages, offer more than one way to find a given page—for example a navigation menu plus a search box, a sitemap, or an A–Z index. Different users navigate differently, so don't force everyone through a single rigid path. Steps within a process, like checkout, are exempt.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities, and anyone who navigates differently, may be unable to find content if the only path to it is one fixed navigation menu.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm at least two ways to reach key pages exist—e.g., navigation plus search, a sitemap, or an index.

codeExamples.label: Navigation plus a search form
codeExamples.code:
<nav aria-label="Primary">{/* menu links */}</nav>
<form role="search" action="/search">
  <label htmlFor="q">Search</label>
  <input id="q" name="q" type="search" />
</form>

codeExamples.label: A single menu is the only way to navigate
codeExamples.code:
{/* No search, sitemap, or index — just one menu */}
<nav aria-label="Primary">{/* menu links */}</nav>

commonMistakes: Providing only a single nav menu with no search, sitemap, or index.
A search box that doesn't actually cover the site's content.
Burying pages so deep they're only reachable through one specific click path.

criteria.title: Headings and Labels
criteria.officialText: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.
criteria.plainEnglish: Headings and form labels must clearly describe the topic or purpose of what they introduce. Vague headings like 'More' or labels like 'Field 1' don't help anyone scan a page or fill out a form. Write descriptive, specific text.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users navigate by headings and rely on labels to fill in forms; vague text like 'More' or 'Field 1' leaves them unable to understand the structure or what to enter.
criteria.quickCheck: Scan your headings and form labels in isolation and confirm each clearly describes the section or field it names.

codeExamples.label: Descriptive heading and label
codeExamples.code: Shipping address

codeExamples.label: Vague heading and label
codeExamples.code: Section 2

commonMistakes: Vague headings like 'Information', 'More', or 'Section 2' that don't describe the content.
Form labels like 'Field 1' or 'Input' that don't say what to enter.
Reusing identical heading text for sections with different content.

criteria.title: Focus Visible
criteria.officialText: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.
criteria.plainEnglish: When a keyboard user tabs to an element, there must be a visible focus indicator showing where they are. Don't remove the browser's default focus outline (outline: none) without providing a clearly visible replacement, or keyboard users get lost.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard users can't tell which element is focused—and so can't navigate at all—when the focus indicator is removed or invisible.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through the page and confirm every focused element shows a clearly visible outline or highlight; search your CSS for 'outline: none' or 'outline: 0'.

codeExamples.label: Clearly visible focus indicator
Focus ring removed with no replacement
codeExamples.code:
/* Keyboard users can no longer see where focus is */
.btn:focus { outline: none; }

commonMistakes: Setting outline: none (or 0) on :focus with no visible replacement style.
A focus style so faint or low-contrast it's hard to see.
Applying focus styles only for mouse interaction, or relying solely on :hover.
Custom controls that never receive a visible focus state.

criteria.title: Location
criteria.officialText: Information about the user's location within a set of web pages is available.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, help users understand where they are within a site—through breadcrumbs, a highlighted current item in the navigation, or a sitemap. Orientation cues prevent people from getting lost in a large site.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities easily get lost in a large site without orientation cues like breadcrumbs or a marked current page.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm the current page is indicated—via breadcrumbs, an aria-current marker in the nav, or a sitemap.

codeExamples.label: Breadcrumb with the current page marked
codeExamples.code:
1. Home
  2. Docs
  3. Forms

codeExamples.label: No location cue anywhere
codeExamples.code:
{/* No breadcrumb or current-page marker */}
Forms

commonMistakes: No breadcrumbs or current-page indicator in deep site hierarchies.
Not marking the active nav item with aria-current="page".
Breadcrumbs that don't reflect the actual page hierarchy.

criteria.title: Link Purpose (Link Only)
criteria.officialText: A mechanism is available to allow the purpose of each link to be identified from link text alone, except where the purpose of the link would be ambiguous to users in general.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the stricter Level AAA version of 2.4.4: each link's purpose must be clear from its link text alone, with no surrounding context needed. And links that share the same text should go to the same place. Write self-describing link text everywhere.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users navigating by link text alone can't distinguish destinations when text is generic, and are misled when identical link text leads to different places.
criteria.quickCheck: Read each link's text completely on its own and confirm it identifies the destination; axe's identical-links-same-purpose flags same-text links that go to different targets.

codeExamples.label: Distinct, self-describing links
codeExamples.code: View pricing plans View enterprise pricing

codeExamples.label: Identical text, different destinations
codeExamples.code: Learn more Learn more

axeRules.description: Ensure that links with the same accessible name serve a similar purpose

commonMistakes: Generic link text ('read more') that needs surrounding context to make sense.
Two links with identical text that lead to different destinations (axe identical-links-same-purpose).
Relying on aria-describedby context instead of making the link text itself descriptive.

criteria.title: Section Headings
criteria.officialText: Section headings are used to organize the content.Note 1: "Heading" is used in its general sense and includes titles and other ways to add a heading to different types of content.Note 2: This success criterion covers sections within writing, not user interface components. User interface components are covered under Success Criterion 4.1.2.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, use headings to organize content into labeled sections. A clear heading structure (properly nested <h1>–<h6>) lets people scan and jump to the part they need, especially in long documents.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users (and anyone skimming) navigate long content by jumping between headings; without section headings they must read everything linearly.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm long content is broken into sections with descriptive headings and that heading levels nest logically with no skipped levels.

codeExamples.label: Content organized with nested headings
codeExamples.code:
Installation

...

Requirements

...

Configuration

...

codeExamples.label: A wall of text with no section headings
codeExamples.code:
{/* One long block with nothing to navigate by */}

Installation... Requirements... Configuration...

commonMistakes: Long pages with no headings to break up the content.
Skipping heading levels (jumping from <h2> to <h4>).
Using headings for visual styling rather than to mark real sections.

criteria.title: Focus Not Obscured (Minimum)
criteria.officialText: When a user interface component receives keyboard focus, the component is not entirely hidden due to author-created content.Note 1: Where content in a configurable interface can be repositioned by the user, then only the initial positions of user-movable content are considered for testing and conformance of this success criterion.Note 2: Content opened by the user may obscure the component receiving focus. If the user can reveal the focused component without advancing the keyboard focus, the component with focus is not considered visually hidden due to author-created content.
criteria.plainEnglish: When an element receives keyboard focus, it must not be entirely hidden behind other content you've added—like a sticky header, footer, or cookie banner. At minimum the focused element has to be at least partially visible so keyboard users can see where they are.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard users lose track of focus when the focused element scrolls completely behind a sticky header, footer, or floating banner.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through the page with sticky headers and footers present and confirm the focused element is never completely hidden behind them.

codeExamples.label: Scroll-margin keeps focus clear of the sticky header
Sticky header hides elements as they get focus
codeExamples.code:
/* No scroll-margin: focused elements scroll under the header */
.site-header { position: sticky; top: 0; height: 6rem; }

commonMistakes: Sticky headers or footers that cover the focused element as you tab down the page.
Cookie banners or chat widgets that overlap focused controls.
Not adding scroll-margin so anchored or focused elements clear fixed overlays.

criteria.title: Focus Not Obscured (Enhanced)
criteria.officialText: When a user interface component receives keyboard focus, no part of the component is hidden by author-created content.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the stricter Level AAA version of 2.4.11: when an element receives keyboard focus, no part of it may be hidden by author content. The entire focused element must be visible, not just partly.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard users can't fully see the focused control—and may miss part of its state or label—if any portion is covered by sticky or floating content.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through the page and confirm the focused element is fully visible, with no part covered by sticky or overlapping content.

codeExamples.label: Focused element is fully clear of overlays
codeExamples.code:
:focus-visible { scroll-margin: 6rem 0; }
.site-header { position: sticky; top: 0; height: 4rem; }

codeExamples.label: Overlay clips part of the focused element

commonMistakes: Sticky elements that partially overlap the focused control (allowed at AA, but not at AAA).
Insufficient scroll-margin, so the top or bottom edge of the focused element is clipped.
Floating chat or help widgets that cover part of the focused control.

criteria.title: Focus Appearance
criteria.officialText: When the keyboard focus indicator is visible, an area of the focus indicator meets all the following:is at least as large as the area of a 2 CSS pixel thick perimeter of the unfocused component or sub-component, andhas a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 between the same pixels in the focused and unfocused states.Exceptions:The focus indicator is determined by the user agent and cannot be adjusted by the author, orThe focus indicator and the indicator's background color are not modified by the author.Note 1: What is perceived as the user interface component or sub-component (to determine the perimeter) depends on its visual presentation. The visual presentation includes the component's visible content, border, and component-specific background. It does not include shadow and glow effects outside the component's content, background, or border.Note 2: Examples of sub-components that may receive a focus indicator are menu items in an opened drop-down menu, or focusable cells in a grid.Note 3: Contrast calculations can be based on colors defined within the technology (such as HTML, CSS, and SVG). Pixels modified by user agent resolution enhancements and anti-aliasing can be ignored.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, the keyboard focus indicator must be substantial: at least as large as a 2px-thick outline around the element, and with at least 3:1 contrast between the focused and unfocused states. A thin, faint focus ring isn't enough—make it large and high-contrast.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with low vision can't perceive a thin or low-contrast focus indicator and lose track of their keyboard position.
criteria.quickCheck: Inspect your focus styles and confirm the indicator is at least 2 CSS px thick (or equivalent area) with at least 3:1 contrast against the unfocused state.

codeExamples.label: Thick, high-contrast focus indicator
Thin, low-contrast focus indicator

commonMistakes: A 1px focus outline that's too thin to meet the minimum area.
A focus color with less than 3:1 contrast against the component's normal state.
Relying on a subtle color shift instead of a clearly visible outline.

criteria.title: Pointer Gestures
criteria.officialText: All functionality that uses multipoint or path-based gestures for operation can be operated with a single pointer without a path-based gesture, unless a multipoint or path-based gesture is essential.Note: This requirement applies to web content that interprets pointer actions (i.e., this does not apply to actions that are required to operate the user agent or assistive technology).
criteria.plainEnglish: Any feature you can operate with a multi-finger or path-based gesture (pinch-zoom, swipe, drag along a path) must also work with a single tap or click. Many people can't perform complex gestures, so provide simple single-pointer alternatives like buttons.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with motor disabilities, or those using a head pointer or switch, can't perform multipoint or path-based gestures like pinch or swipe and need a single-pointer alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: For every swipe, pinch, or path gesture, confirm there's a simple single-tap/click control (such as buttons) that does the same thing.

codeExamples.label: Swipe plus single-pointer buttons
Advancing requires a swipe gesture
codeExamples.code:
{/* Only advances via touch swipe — no button alternative */}
{/* slides */}

commonMistakes: Carousels or galleries that can only be advanced by swiping, with no previous/next buttons.
Pinch-to-zoom as the only way to zoom, with no zoom buttons.
Path-based gestures (like drawing a shape) with no single-pointer equivalent.

criteria.title: Pointer Cancellation
criteria.officialText: For functionality that can be operated using a single pointer, at least one of the following is true:No Down-Event: The down-event of the pointer is not used to execute any part of the function;Abort or Undo: Completion of the function is on the up-event, and a mechanism is available to abort the function before completion or to undo the function after completion;Up Reversal: The up-event reverses any outcome of the preceding down-event;Essential: Completing the function on the down-event is essential.Note 1: Functions that emulate a keyboard or numeric keypad key press are considered essential.Note 2: This requirement applies to web content that interprets pointer actions (i.e., this does not apply to actions that are required to operate the user agent or assistive technology).
criteria.plainEnglish: Actions should fire on the up-event (release), not the down-event (press), so a user who presses by mistake can slide off the target to cancel. Alternatively, provide undo. This lets people with motor difficulties recover from accidental presses.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with motor disabilities who press a control by accident can't cancel the action if it fires immediately on the down-press instead of on release.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm actions trigger on the click/up-event (not mousedown/touchstart) so users can drag off to abort, or that an undo is available.

codeExamples.label: Fires on release, so it can be aborted
codeExamples.code:
{/* onClick fires on the up-event; slide off to cancel */}
<button onClick={deleteItem}>Delete</button>

codeExamples.label: Fires on press with no chance to cancel
codeExamples.code:
{/* Commits the instant it is pressed */}
<button onMouseDown={deleteItem}>Delete</button>

commonMistakes: Triggering actions on onMouseDown or onTouchStart instead of onClick (the up-event).
Providing no way to abort an action by moving off the control before releasing.
Drag actions that commit on the down-event with no undo.

criteria.title: Label in Name
criteria.officialText: For user interface components with labels that include text or images of text, the name contains the text that is presented visually.Note: A best practice is to have the text of the label at the start of the name.
criteria.plainEnglish: A control's accessible name must include the visible text label users see. If a button shows 'Search' but its aria-label is 'Submit', speech-input users who say 'click Search' can't activate it. Keep the visible text and the accessible name in sync.
criteria.whyItMatters: Speech-input users say a control's visible label to activate it; if the accessible name doesn't contain that visible text, the voice command fails.
criteria.quickCheck: Compare each control's visible text to its accessible name and confirm the visible text is included; axe's label-content-name-mismatch rule flags mismatches.

codeExamples.label: Accessible name contains the visible text
codeExamples.code:
{/* Name "Search products" starts with the visible "Search" */}
<button aria-label="Search products">Search</button>

codeExamples.label: Accessible name doesn't match visible text
codeExamples.code:
{/* Visible text says "Search" but the name is "Submit" */}
<button aria-label="Submit">Search</button>

axeRules.description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name

commonMistakes: An aria-label that replaces the visible text with different wording (axe label-content-name-mismatch).
A button showing 'Search' but named 'Submit' or 'Go' via aria-label.
Icon-plus-text buttons whose aria-label omits the visible word.
Visible label text that isn't part of the computed accessible name.

criteria.title: Motion Actuation
criteria.officialText: Functionality that can be operated by device motion or user motion can also be operated by user interface components and responding to the motion can be disabled to prevent accidental actuation, except when:Supported Interface: The motion is used to operate functionality through an accessibility supported interface;Essential: The motion is essential for the function and doing so would invalidate the activity.
criteria.plainEnglish: If a feature can be triggered by moving the device (shake to undo) or by user motion (tilt), also provide a regular UI control for it, and let users disable motion triggering. Otherwise people who can't move the device—or who move it involuntarily—are excluded or trigger things by accident.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with motor or mobility disabilities who can't shake or tilt a device—or who move it involuntarily—need a UI-control alternative and a way to disable motion triggers.
criteria.quickCheck: For any shake/tilt/motion feature, confirm there's an on-screen control that does the same thing and a setting to disable motion actuation.

codeExamples.label: Motion shortcut plus an on-screen button
Action only available via device motion
codeExamples.code:
{/* Undo only possible by physically shaking the device */}
window.addEventListener("devicemotion", maybeUndoOnShake);

commonMistakes: 'Shake to undo' or tilt-to-scroll with no equivalent on-screen button.
No setting to turn off motion-based actuation, causing accidental triggers.
Relying on device motion as the only way to perform an action.

criteria.title: Target Size (Enhanced)
criteria.officialText: The size of the target for pointer inputs is at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels except when:Equivalent: The target is available through an equivalent link or control on the same page that is at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels;Inline: The target is in a sentence or block of text;User Agent Control: The size of the target is determined by the user agent and is not modified by the author;Essential: A particular presentation of the target is essential to the information being conveyed.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, tap and click targets should be at least 44×44 CSS pixels, with some exceptions for inline links and equivalent controls. Larger targets are much easier to hit for people with motor difficulties or anyone using touch.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with motor disabilities or tremors, and touchscreen users, frequently miss targets smaller than 44×44 pixels.
criteria.quickCheck: Measure interactive targets in DevTools and confirm each is at least 44×44 CSS pixels, or qualifies for an exception.

codeExamples.label: Target meets the 44px enhanced size
Target below the 44px enhanced size

commonMistakes: Icon buttons or close (×) buttons smaller than 44×44px.
Tightly packed small tap targets with no padding to enlarge the hit area.
Relying on a tiny visual icon without expanding its clickable region.

criteria.title: Concurrent Input Mechanisms
criteria.officialText: Web content does not restrict use of input modalities available on a platform except where the restriction is essential, required to ensure the security of the content, or required to respect user settings.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, don't lock users into one input method. People may switch between touch, mouse, keyboard, and stylus—even mid-task—so your content shouldn't restrict which input modalities work, unless the restriction is essential or needed for security.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users who switch input methods—for example from touch to an attached keyboard—are blocked when content is hard-coded to accept only one modality.
criteria.quickCheck: Try operating the app with touch, mouse, and keyboard interchangeably and confirm none are artificially disabled.

codeExamples.label: Supports pointer and keyboard regardless of device
codeExamples.code:
<div
  onPointerDown={handleActivate}
  onKeyDown={(e) => e.key === "Enter" && handleActivate(e)}
  tabIndex={0}
  role="button"
/>

codeExamples.label: Disables keyboard when touch is detected
codeExamples.code:
// Removes keyboard handling on touch devices
if (isTouchDevice) element.onkeydown = null;

commonMistakes: Detecting a 'touch device' and then disabling mouse or keyboard handlers.
Building interactions that assume only one input type will ever be used.
Removing hover or keyboard support on touch devices.

criteria.title: Dragging Movements
criteria.officialText: All functionality that uses a dragging movement for operation can be achieved by a single pointer without dragging, unless dragging is essential or the functionality is determined by the user agent and not modified by the author.Note: This requirement applies to web content that interprets pointer actions (i.e., this does not apply to actions that are required to operate the user agent or assistive technology).
criteria.plainEnglish: Any feature that uses dragging—reordering a list, a slider, drag-and-drop—must also be operable with a single pointer without dragging, for example tap-to-select then tap-destination, or up/down buttons. Dragging is hard for many people with motor disabilities.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with motor disabilities, tremors, or who use a head pointer often can't perform a sustained drag and need a no-drag alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: For every drag interaction (reorder, slider, drag-drop), confirm there's a no-drag alternative such as click-to-move buttons or tap targets.

codeExamples.label: Reorder via buttons, not just dragging
codeExamples.code: • Task 1

codeExamples.label: Reordering only possible by dragging
codeExamples.code:
{/* Cards can only be reordered by dragging */}
  • Task 1

commonMistakes: Drag-and-drop reordering with no up/down buttons or other alternative.
Sliders that can only be set by dragging the thumb, with no clickable track or text input.
Kanban-style boards where cards can only be moved by dragging.

criteria.title: Target Size (Minimum)
criteria.officialText: The size of the target for pointer inputs is at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels, except when:Spacing: Undersized targets (those less than 24 by 24 CSS pixels) are positioned so that if a 24 CSS pixel diameter circle is centered on the bounding box of each, the circles do not intersect another target or the circle for another undersized target;Equivalent: The function can be achieved through a different control on the same page that meets this criterion;Inline: The target is in a sentence or its size is otherwise constrained by the line-height of non-target text;User Agent Control: The size of the target is determined by the user agent and is not modified by the author;Essential: A particular presentation of the target is essential or is legally required for the information being conveyed.Note 1: Targets that allow for values to be selected spatially based on position within the target are considered one target for the purpose of the success criterion. Examples include sliders, color pickers displaying a gradient of colors, or editable areas where you position the cursor.Note 2: For inline targets the line-height should be interpreted as perpendicular to the flow of text. For example, in a language displayed vertically, the line-height would be horizontal.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AA, tap and click targets must be at least 24×24 CSS pixels, or have enough spacing that a 24px circle centered on each doesn't overlap a neighbor. Tiny, crowded controls are hard to hit accurately, especially on touchscreens.
criteria.whyItMatters: Touchscreen users and people with motor disabilities or tremors mis-tap controls that are smaller than 24×24px or packed too closely together.
criteria.quickCheck: Measure interactive targets in DevTools and confirm each is at least 24×24 CSS pixels or has adequate spacing; axe's target-size rule flags small ones.

codeExamples.label: Target meets the 24px minimum size
Target below the 24px minimum size

axeRules.description: Ensure touch targets have sufficient size and space

commonMistakes: Buttons, links, or icons smaller than 24×24px (axe target-size).
Adjacent small targets with no spacing between them.
Tiny close (×) buttons or icon-only toolbar buttons.
Small inline checkboxes or radios with cramped clickable areas.

criteria.title: Language of Page
criteria.officialText: The default human language of each web page can be programmatically determined.
criteria.plainEnglish: Set the page's default language with a lang attribute on the <html> element (e.g., lang="en"). Screen readers use it to choose the right pronunciation rules and voice; without it, English text might be read with a French accent and vice versa.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen readers mispronounce an entire page—making it hard to understand—when the <html> element has no valid lang attribute telling them which language to speak.
criteria.quickCheck: Inspect the <html> element and confirm it has a valid lang attribute (e.g., lang="en"); axe's html-has-lang and html-lang-valid rules check this.

codeExamples.label: Document declares its language
codeExamples.code: <html lang="en">

codeExamples.label: Document with no lang attribute
codeExamples.code: <html>

axeRules.description: Ensure every HTML document has a lang attribute
Ensure the lang attribute of the <html> element has a valid value
Ensure that HTML elements with both valid lang and xml:lang attributes agree on the base language of the page

commonMistakes: Omitting the lang attribute on the <html> element entirely (axe html-has-lang).
Using an invalid or made-up language code like lang="english" instead of lang="en" (axe html-lang-valid).
lang and xml:lang attributes that disagree on the page's language (axe html-xml-lang-mismatch).
Hard-coding lang="en" on a site that serves multiple languages instead of setting it per locale.

criteria.title: Language of Parts
criteria.officialText: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined except for proper names, technical terms, words of indeterminate language, and words or phrases that have become part of the vernacular of the immediately surrounding text.
criteria.plainEnglish: When a passage or phrase is in a different language from the page, mark it with a lang attribute on that element (e.g., <span lang="fr">). Screen readers then switch pronunciation for that part. Proper names and words absorbed into the surrounding language are exempt.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen readers mispronounce foreign-language phrases—like a French quote on an English page—unless that phrase is marked with its own lang attribute.
criteria.quickCheck: Find any foreign-language words or quotes and confirm each is wrapped in an element with a valid lang attribute; axe's valid-lang rule flags invalid values.

codeExamples.label: Foreign phrase marked with its language
codeExamples.code: The chef called it haute cuisine.

codeExamples.label: Foreign phrase with no language marker
codeExamples.code: The chef called it haute cuisine.

axeRules.description: Ensure lang attributes have valid values

commonMistakes: Not marking inline foreign-language quotes or phrases with a lang attribute.
Using an invalid language code in a lang attribute (axe valid-lang).
Marking proper names or common loanwords that don't actually need a language change.

criteria.title: Unusual Words
criteria.officialText: A mechanism is available for identifying specific definitions of words or phrases used in an unusual or restricted way, including idioms and jargon.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, provide a way to look up definitions for words used in an unusual or restricted way—jargon, idioms, technical terms. A glossary, inline definitions, or <dfn> markup helps people who struggle with non-literal or specialized language.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities, or anyone unfamiliar with the domain, can't understand jargon and idioms unless definitions are made available.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm jargon, idioms, and specialized terms link to or expose a definition, such as a glossary entry or an inline <dfn>.

codeExamples.label: Unusual term linked to a definition
codeExamples.code: The request was idempotent — safe to retry. (definition)

codeExamples.label: Jargon with no definition mechanism
codeExamples.code: The request was idempotent — safe to retry.

commonMistakes: Using domain jargon or idioms with no glossary or inline definitions.
Defining a term only once and never linking later uses back to it.
Assuming all readers share your domain vocabulary.

criteria.title: Abbreviations
criteria.officialText: A mechanism for identifying the expanded form or meaning of abbreviations is available.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, give users a way to find the expanded form of abbreviations and acronyms. Expand it on first use, or use the <abbr title="..."> element so the full term is available. This helps people who don't recognize the shorthand.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive or reading disabilities, and newcomers to a field, can't decode abbreviations like 'ARIA' or 'SLA' unless the expansion is provided.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm abbreviations are expanded on first use or wrapped in <abbr title="full form">.

codeExamples.label: Abbreviation with its expansion available
codeExamples.code: Add an ARIA role.

codeExamples.label: Bare abbreviation with no expansion
codeExamples.code: Add an ARIA role.

commonMistakes: Using acronyms with no expansion on first use and no <abbr> markup.
Relying only on the <abbr> title tooltip, which isn't reachable by keyboard or touch — expand it in text too.
Assuming an abbreviation is universally understood.

criteria.title: Reading Level
criteria.officialText: When text requires reading ability more advanced than the lower secondary education level after removal of proper names and titles, supplemental content, or a version that does not require reading ability more advanced than the lower secondary education level, is available.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, if your text requires reading ability beyond roughly the lower-secondary (about 9th-grade) level, provide a simpler alternative or supplemental content such as summaries or illustrations. Aim to write plainly so more people can understand.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with reading disabilities or limited literacy can't understand content written above a lower-secondary reading level without a simpler alternative.
criteria.quickCheck: Run your text through a readability checker (e.g., Hemingway or a Flesch–Kincaid tool); if it's above ~9th-grade level, provide a plain-language summary.

codeExamples.label: Plain-language summary alongside the detail
codeExamples.code:
In short: you can cancel any time, no fee.

Subscribers may terminate the agreement at their sole discretion without incurring early-termination penalties...

codeExamples.label: Dense legal prose with no simpler version
codeExamples.code: Subscribers may terminate the agreement at their sole discretion without incurring early-termination penalties...

commonMistakes: Dense, jargon-heavy prose with no plain-language summary.
Long, complex sentences where short ones would do.
No summaries, illustrations, or supplemental content for difficult material.

criteria.title: Pronunciation
criteria.officialText: A mechanism is available for identifying specific pronunciation of words where meaning of the words, in context, is ambiguous without knowing the pronunciation.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, where a word's meaning depends on how it's pronounced (heteronyms like 'lead' the metal vs. 'lead' the verb), provide a way to convey the pronunciation—phonetic spelling, ruby annotations, or audio. This matters most where pronunciation disambiguates meaning.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users and people with reading disabilities can misunderstand words whose meaning changes with pronunciation (like 'desert' the place vs. 'desert' the verb) without a pronunciation cue.
criteria.quickCheck: Identify words whose meaning depends on pronunciation and confirm a cue is provided—phonetic spelling, ruby text, or audio.

codeExamples.label: Pronunciation conveyed with ruby text
codeExamples.code: I will lead/liːd/ the team.

codeExamples.label: Ambiguous word with no pronunciation cue
codeExamples.code: I will lead the team.

commonMistakes: Using heteronyms ambiguously with no pronunciation guidance.
Omitting ruby annotations where a writing system relies on them.
Assuming context alone resolves an ambiguous pronunciation.

criteria.title: On Focus
criteria.officialText: When any user interface component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.
criteria.plainEnglish: Moving keyboard focus to an element must not trigger an unexpected change of context—like submitting a form, opening a new window, or navigating away. Focusing a field should never surprise the user; wait for an explicit action (a click or Enter) to make big changes.
criteria.whyItMatters: Keyboard and screen reader users get disoriented when simply tabbing onto a control unexpectedly navigates away or opens a new window.
criteria.quickCheck: Tab through every control and confirm that merely focusing it never submits a form, opens a window, or navigates — nothing should change context on focus alone.

codeExamples.label: Focus alone changes nothing
codeExamples.code:
{/* Focus just focuses — navigation happens only on explicit submit */}
<select onChange={handleChange}>{/* options */}</select>

codeExamples.label: Focusing the field navigates away
codeExamples.code:
{/* Tabbing onto the field unexpectedly changes context */}
<select onFocus={() => (window.location.href = "/next")}>{/* options */}</select>

commonMistakes: Using onFocus to auto-submit a form or navigate to a new page.
Opening a popup or new window when a field receives focus.
Auto-advancing to the next field on focus rather than after input is complete.

criteria.title: On Input
criteria.officialText: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised of the behavior before using the component.
criteria.plainEnglish: Changing a form control's value—selecting a radio, picking from a dropdown, typing—must not automatically cause a change of context unless you've warned the user first. Don't auto-submit a form when a select changes; use an explicit submit button instead.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader and keyboard users are thrown off when selecting a dropdown option auto-submits or navigates before they've finished, with no warning.
criteria.quickCheck: Change each form control's value and confirm nothing auto-submits or navigates unless the user was clearly warned in advance.

codeExamples.label: Selection plus an explicit Go button
codeExamples.code:
<select value={page} onChange={(e) => setPage(e.target.value)}>{/* options */}</select>
<button onClick={() => navigate(page)}>Go</button>

codeExamples.label: Selection navigates with no warning
codeExamples.code:
{/* Navigates the instant a different option is chosen */}
<select onChange={(e) => navigate(e.target.value)}>{/* options */}</select>

commonMistakes: Auto-submitting or navigating in a select's onChange (a 'jump to' menu) with no warning or Go button.
Reloading the page when a checkbox or radio changes.
Advancing a wizard step the instant an option is selected.

criteria.title: Consistent Navigation
criteria.officialText: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple web pages within a set of web pages occur in the same relative order each time they are repeated, unless a change is initiated by the user.
criteria.plainEnglish: Navigation that repeats across pages—main menu, search, breadcrumbs—must appear in the same relative order on every page. Users learn where things are; shuffling the navigation order between pages forces them to relearn it each time.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users and people with cognitive disabilities rely on navigation being in a predictable place; reordering it between pages forces them to re-find everything.
criteria.quickCheck: Compare several pages and confirm repeated navigation items appear in the same relative order on each.

codeExamples.label: One shared Nav renders the same order everywhere
codeExamples.code:
{/* A single shared component keeps order consistent across pages */}
<Layout>
  <Nav />
  <main>{children}</main>
</Layout>

codeExamples.label: Navigation order differs per page
codeExamples.code:
{/* Home lists Products first; About lists Contact first */}
<nav>{page === "home" ? <Products /> : <Contact />}</nav>

commonMistakes: Reordering menu items differently from page to page.
Moving the search box or nav to a different position on some pages.
Conditionally hiding or reordering nav links inconsistently across templates.

criteria.title: Consistent Identification
criteria.officialText: Components that have the same functionality within a set of web pages are identified consistently.
criteria.plainEnglish: Components with the same function across pages must be identified consistently—same label, same icon, same accessible name. If a search button says 'Search' on one page and 'Find' on another, users (especially screen reader users) can't tell they do the same thing.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users identify controls by their name; labeling the same function differently across pages (e.g., 'Search' vs. 'Find') makes them seem like different features.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm controls with the same function use the same label, icon, and accessible name everywhere they appear.

codeExamples.label: Same label for the same action everywhere
codeExamples.code:
{/* This action is labeled identically on every page */}
<button>Download PDF</button>

codeExamples.label: Same action labeled differently per page
codeExamples.code:
<button>Download PDF</button>   {/* page A */}
<button>Get the file</button>  {/* page B */}

commonMistakes: Labeling the same action differently across pages ('Search' vs. 'Find', 'Submit' vs. 'Send').
Using different icons for the same function in different places.
Inconsistent accessible names (aria-label) for identical controls.

criteria.title: Change on Request
criteria.officialText: Changes of context are initiated only by user request or a mechanism is available to turn off such changes.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, changes of context—navigation, new windows, major content swaps—happen only when the user explicitly requests them, or you provide a way to turn automatic changes off. No surprise redirects, auto-refreshes, or pop-ups.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities and screen reader users lose their place when the page redirects, refreshes, or opens windows automatically without their request.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm context changes happen only on explicit user action, and check there's no <meta http-equiv="refresh"> auto-redirect (axe meta-refresh-no-exceptions).

codeExamples.label: Context changes only on a user click
codeExamples.code:
{/* Navigation happens only when the user chooses */}
<button onClick={() => navigate("/dashboard")}>Go to dashboard</button>

codeExamples.label: Automatic redirect, not user-initiated
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Redirects on a timer rather than on user request -->
<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="5; url=/dashboard" />

axeRules.description: Ensure <meta http-equiv="refresh"> is not used for delayed refresh

commonMistakes: Auto-refreshing or auto-redirecting with <meta http-equiv="refresh"> (axe meta-refresh-no-exceptions).
Opening new windows or popups the user didn't request.
Automatically advancing carousels or content in a way that changes context.

criteria.title: Consistent Help
criteria.officialText: If a web page contains any of the following help mechanisms, and those mechanisms are repeated on multiple web pages within a set of web pages, they occur in the same order relative to other page content, unless a change is initiated by the user:Human contact details;Human contact mechanism;Self-help option;A fully automated contact mechanism.Note 1: Help mechanisms may be provided directly on the page, or may be provided via a direct link to a different page containing the information.Note 2: For this success criterion, "the same order relative to other page content" can be thought of as how the content is ordered when the page is serialized. The visual position of a help mechanism is likely to be consistent across pages for the same page variation (e.g., CSS break-point). The user can initiate a change, such as changing the page's zoom or orientation, which may trigger a different page variation. This criterion is concerned with relative order across pages displayed in the same page variation (e.g., same zoom level and orientation).
criteria.plainEnglish: If your pages offer help mechanisms—contact details, a help link, a chat widget—and they repeat across pages, keep them in the same relative location and order on every page. Users in difficulty shouldn't have to hunt for help in a different spot each time.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities who need help can't find it if the contact link or help widget moves to a different place on each page.
criteria.quickCheck: Compare pages and confirm help mechanisms (contact link, chat, help) appear in the same relative order and location on each.

codeExamples.label: Help link in the same footer slot on every page
codeExamples.code:
{/* Rendered in the same place site-wide */}
Contact support

codeExamples.label: Help link placed inconsistently across pages
codeExamples.code:
{/* In the header here, buried mid-page elsewhere */}
Contact support

commonMistakes: Placing the help or contact link in a different spot on different pages.
Showing a chat or help widget on some pages but not others, inconsistently.
Reordering help options relative to other content between templates.

criteria.title: Error Identification
criteria.officialText: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.
criteria.plainEnglish: When a form input error is detected, tell the user in text which field is wrong and what's wrong with it. Don't rely on color alone (a red border)—say 'Email address is required' in words, and associate the message with the field programmatically (aria-describedby, aria-invalid).
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users and colorblind users can't tell which field failed, or why, when an error is shown only with a red outline and no text.
criteria.quickCheck: Submit a form with bad data and confirm each error is described in text and associated with its field (aria-invalid, aria-describedby); test with a screen reader.

codeExamples.label: Error described in text and tied to the field
codeExamples.code: Enter a valid email address.

codeExamples.label: Error shown only as a red border
codeExamples.code:
{/* No text, no aria-invalid — invisible to screen readers */}
<input className="error" />

commonMistakes: Indicating errors only with a red border and no text message.
Not linking the error text to the field with aria-describedby.
Not setting aria-invalid on the field that's in error.
A generic 'the form has errors' message that doesn't say which field.

criteria.title: Labels or Instructions
criteria.officialText: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
criteria.plainEnglish: Every input that requires user data needs a clear label or instructions telling people what to enter. Use a real <label> tied to the field, and add format hints (like 'MM/DD/YYYY') where needed. Placeholders alone don't count—they vanish as soon as the user types.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users can't fill in a field that has no associated label, and everyone struggles when required formats (date, password rules) aren't stated.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm every input has a programmatically associated <label> plus any needed format instructions; axe's form-field-multiple-labels flags fields with conflicting labels.

codeExamples.label: Associated label plus a format hint
codeExamples.code: Format: MM/DD/YYYY

codeExamples.label: Placeholder used instead of a label
codeExamples.code: <input placeholder="Date of birth (MM/DD/YYYY)" />

axeRules.description: Ensure form field does not have multiple label elements

commonMistakes: Using placeholder text as the only label, which disappears once the user types.
No instructions for required formats (date format, password rules).
Multiple <label> elements on one field, confusing its accessible name (axe form-field-multiple-labels).
A label not associated with its input (missing htmlFor/id pairing).

criteria.title: Error Suggestion
criteria.officialText: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestions are provided to the user, unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.
criteria.plainEnglish: When you detect an input error and you know how to fix it, suggest the correction—not just 'invalid input', but 'Email must include an @' or the expected format. Help users recover instead of leaving them guessing. (Unless suggesting would hurt security, as with passwords.)
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities—and really everyone—get stuck repeating the same mistake when an error says only 'invalid' without suggesting how to fix it.
criteria.quickCheck: Trigger validation errors and confirm the messages suggest a concrete fix (the expected format or valid range), not just 'invalid'.

codeExamples.label: Error message suggests the fix
codeExamples.code: Enter the date as MM/DD/YYYY — for example, 04/23/2025.

codeExamples.label: Error message offers no guidance
codeExamples.code: Invalid input.

commonMistakes: Showing 'Invalid input' with no hint about what's expected.
Not suggesting a correction when the expected format is known (e.g., a date or phone format).
Generic messages that don't point to the specific problem.

criteria.title: Error Prevention (Legal, Financial, Data)
criteria.officialText: For web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions for the user to occur, that modify or delete user-controllable data in data storage systems, or that submit user test responses, at least one of the following is true:Reversible: Submissions are reversible.Checked: Data entered by the user is checked for input errors and the user is provided an opportunity to correct them.Confirmed: A mechanism is available for reviewing, confirming, and correcting information before finalizing the submission.
criteria.plainEnglish: For pages that create legal or financial commitments, modify or delete user data, or submit test answers, provide a safety net: make submissions reversible, check inputs for errors, or let users review and confirm before finalizing. This prevents costly mistakes on high-stakes actions.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with disabilities—and anyone—can make irreversible, costly mistakes (a wrong payment, a deleted account) when there's no review, confirmation, or undo step.
criteria.quickCheck: For checkout, account changes, or deletions, confirm there's a review/confirm step, input checking, or a way to reverse the action.

codeExamples.label: Review-and-confirm before an irreversible action
codeExamples.code:
<button onClick={() => setStep("review")}>Review order</button>
{step === "review" && (
  <button onClick={placeOrder}>Confirm purchase</button>
)}

codeExamples.label: Charges immediately with no confirmation
codeExamples.code:
{/* No review step before an irreversible charge */}
<button onClick={placeOrder}>Buy now</button>

commonMistakes: Finalizing a payment or legal agreement with no review-and-confirm step.
Permanently deleting data with no confirmation or undo.
Not re-checking critical inputs before submission.

criteria.title: Help
criteria.officialText: Context-sensitive help is available.
criteria.plainEnglish: At Level AAA, provide context-sensitive help—inline hints, a help link near complex fields, examples, or access to support. People who get stuck on a form or task should be able to get guidance without leaving the page.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities may be unable to complete a complex form or task without context-sensitive help available right where they're working.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm complex forms and tasks offer context-sensitive help—inline hints, examples, or a help link near the relevant fields.

codeExamples.label: Inline help next to a complex field
codeExamples.code: Find this on line 2 of your W-9. Need help?

codeExamples.label: Complex field with no help available
codeExamples.code:
<label htmlFor="tax-id">Tax ID</label>
<input id="tax-id" />

commonMistakes: Complex forms with no inline help, examples, or hints.
Burying help on a separate page far from where it's needed.
Offering no way to reach human support when self-help isn't enough.

criteria.title: Error Prevention (All)
criteria.officialText: For web pages that require the user to submit information, at least one of the following is true:Reversible: Submissions are reversible.Checked: Data entered by the user is checked for input errors and the user is provided an opportunity to correct them.Confirmed: A mechanism is available for reviewing, confirming, and correcting information before finalizing the submission.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the Level AAA version of 3.3.4, applied to all forms that submit information—not just legal, financial, or data-changing ones. Any submission should be reversible, error-checked, or confirmable before finalizing. Give every form a safety net.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with disabilities can make submission mistakes on any form, not just high-stakes ones, when there's no error check, confirmation, or way to reverse it.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm every form that submits information offers error checking, a review/confirm step, or reversibility.

codeExamples.label: Validate, then confirm before submitting
codeExamples.code:
{/* A safety net on an ordinary form, not just checkout */}
<button onClick={validateThenConfirm}>Review and submit</button>

codeExamples.label: Form submits immediately with no checks
codeExamples.code:
{/* No validation or confirmation */}
<form onSubmit={submitNow}>{/* fields */}</form>

commonMistakes: Submitting any form with no validation or confirmation step.
Providing no way to edit or undo a submission after sending.
Assuming only payment forms need a safety net.

criteria.title: Redundant Entry
criteria.officialText: Information previously entered by or provided to the user that is required to be entered again in the same process is either:auto-populated, oravailable for the user to select.Except when:re-entering the information is essential,the information is required to ensure the security of the content, orpreviously entered information is no longer valid.
criteria.plainEnglish: Don't make users re-enter information they already provided earlier in the same process. Auto-populate it, or let them pick it from a previous answer (like a 'same as billing address' checkbox). Exceptions: when re-entry is essential or for security (e.g., confirming a password).
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities or motor impairments are burdened by re-typing the same information—like an address—multiple times in one flow.
criteria.quickCheck: Walk through a multi-step flow and confirm previously entered info is auto-filled or selectable, not re-typed (except passwords or genuinely essential re-entry).

codeExamples.label: Reuse earlier input with a 'same as' option
codeExamples.code:
<label>
  <input type="checkbox" onChange={copyBillingToShipping} />
  Shipping address same as billing
</label>

codeExamples.label: Forces re-entry of the same data

commonMistakes: Asking for the same address or email again later in the same flow with no auto-fill.
Providing no 'same as above' option to copy previously entered data.
Clearing earlier answers and forcing re-entry after a validation error.

criteria.title: Accessible Authentication (Minimum)
criteria.officialText: A cognitive function test (such as remembering a password or solving a puzzle) is not required for any step in an authentication process unless that step provides at least one of the following:Alternative: Another authentication method that does not rely on a cognitive function test.Mechanism: A mechanism is available to assist the user in completing the cognitive function test.Object Recognition: The cognitive function test is to recognize objects.Personal Content: The cognitive function test is to identify non-text content the user provided to the website.Note 1: "Object recognition" and "Personal content" may be represented by images, video, or audio.Note 2: Examples of mechanisms that satisfy this criterion include:support for password entry by password managers to reduce memory need, andcopy and paste to reduce the cognitive burden of re-typing.
criteria.plainEnglish: Don't require a cognitive function test—memorizing a password, solving a puzzle, transcribing characters—to log in, unless you offer an alternative or assistance. Allow password managers and copy-paste, support passkeys or email links, and don't block paste on password fields.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities (memory, attention) are locked out by logins that require remembering passwords or solving puzzles with no assistance like password-manager support.
criteria.quickCheck: Try logging in with a password manager and by pasting into the password field; confirm neither is blocked and that any puzzle/CAPTCHA has an accessible alternative.

codeExamples.label: Password field that managers can fill and users can paste
codeExamples.code:
{/* autocomplete lets password managers fill; paste is allowed */}
<input type="password" name="password" autoComplete="current-password" />

codeExamples.label: Password field that blocks paste and autofill
codeExamples.code:
<input
  type="password"
  onPaste={(e) => e.preventDefault()}
  autoComplete="off"
/>

commonMistakes: Blocking paste (onPaste preventDefault) on password or one-time-code fields.
Requiring a CAPTCHA or puzzle with no non-cognitive alternative.
Disabling autocomplete so password managers can't fill credentials.
Requiring users to manually transcribe a code from one place to another.

criteria.title: Accessible Authentication (Enhanced)
criteria.officialText: A cognitive function test (such as remembering a password or solving a puzzle) is not required for any step in an authentication process unless that step provides at least one of the following:Alternative: Another authentication method that does not rely on a cognitive function test.Mechanism: A mechanism is available to assist the user in completing the cognitive function test.
criteria.plainEnglish: This is the stricter Level AAA version of 3.3.8: a cognitive function test can't be required at any authentication step unless there's a non-cognitive alternative or an assisting mechanism—and the object-recognition and personal-content exceptions of 3.3.8 are removed. Prefer passwordless methods like passkeys.
criteria.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive disabilities are excluded by any login step requiring memory or puzzle-solving, even the object-recognition CAPTCHAs that are still allowed at Level AA.
criteria.quickCheck: Confirm authentication offers a method with no cognitive test at all—such as passkeys, email magic links, or full password-manager support.

codeExamples.label: Passwordless sign-in with no cognitive test
codeExamples.code:
{/* Passkeys require no memory or puzzle-solving */}
<button onClick={signInWithPasskey}>Sign in with a passkey</button>

codeExamples.label: Login gated behind a puzzle CAPTCHA
codeExamples.code:
{/* Requires solving a puzzle to authenticate */}
<PuzzleCaptcha onSolve={continueLogin} />

commonMistakes: Requiring an object-recognition CAPTCHA (allowed at AA but not at AAA).
Offering only password-based login with no passwordless alternative.
Blocking password managers or paste on credential fields.

criteria.title: Parsing
criteria.officialText: In content implemented using markup languages, elements have complete start and end tags, elements are nested according to their specifications, elements do not contain duplicate attributes, and any IDs are unique, except where the specifications allow these features.Note 1: This success criterion should be considered as always satisfied for any content using HTML or XML.Note 2:Since this criterion was written, the HTML Living Standard has adopted specific requirements governing how user agents must handle incomplete tags, incorrect element nesting, duplicate attributes, and non-unique IDs. [HTML]Although the HTML standard treats some of these cases as non-conforming for authors, it is considered to "allow these features" for the purposes of this success criterion because the specification requires that user agents support handling these cases consistently. In practice, this criterion no longer provides any benefit to people with disabilities in itself.Issues such as missing roles due to inappropriately nested elements or incorrect states or names due to a duplicate ID are covered by different success criteria and should be reported under those criteria rather than as issues with 4.1.1.
criteria.plainEnglish: Markup should be well-formed: complete tags, proper nesting, no duplicate attributes, and unique IDs. Note: in WCAG 2.2 this criterion is considered always satisfied for HTML/XML because browsers handle these cases—but duplicate IDs can still break ARIA references, so keep IDs unique.
criteria.whyItMatters: Duplicate IDs can break ARIA relationships (like aria-labelledby pointing at the wrong element), so assistive tech announces the wrong name or skips content.
criteria.quickCheck: Validate your HTML and confirm IDs are unique (axe's duplicate-id rules); broken nesting and duplicate attributes are largely handled by browsers now.

codeExamples.label: Unique id per rendered item
Duplicate ids break label and ARIA refs

axeRules.description: Ensure every id attribute value of active elements is unique
Ensure every id attribute value is unique

commonMistakes: Reusing the same id on multiple elements, breaking label or ARIA associations (axe duplicate-id).
Duplicate ids on active or focusable elements (axe duplicate-id-active).
Rendering a list where every item hard-codes the same id instead of a unique one.
Pointing aria-labelledby or htmlFor at an id that appears more than once.

criteria.title: Name, Role, Value
criteria.officialText: For all user interface components (including but not limited to: form elements, links and components generated by scripts), the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values that can be set by the user can be programmatically set; and notification of changes to these items is available to user agents, including assistive technologies.Note: This success criterion is primarily for web authors who develop or script their own user interface components. For example, standard HTML controls already meet this success criterion when used according to specification.
criteria.plainEnglish: Every UI component must expose its name (what it's called), role (what kind of thing it is—button, checkbox, tab), and value/state (checked, expanded, selected) to assistive tech, and notify them when these change. Native HTML elements do this for free; custom widgets built from <div>s need the right ARIA roles, properties, and states.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users can't understand or operate a custom control built from <div>s—they don't hear what it is, what it's called, or whether it's on or off—unless its name, role, and state are exposed.
criteria.quickCheck: Inspect custom widgets in the Chrome DevTools Accessibility pane and confirm each exposes a name, a correct role, and current state; axe flags missing names (button-name, link-name, label) and invalid ARIA.

codeExamples.label: Native button exposes name, role, and state
codeExamples.code:
{/* aria-pressed exposes the toggle state; the role and name are built in */}
<button aria-pressed={on} onClick={toggle}>Mute</button>

codeExamples.label: Div toggle exposes nothing to assistive tech
codeExamples.code:
{/* No role, no name, no state */}
<div className="toggle" onClick={toggle} />

axeRules.description: Ensure <area> elements of image maps have alternative text
Ensure an element's role supports its ARIA attributes
Ensure aria-braillelabel and aria-brailleroledescription have a non-braille equivalent
Ensure every ARIA button, link and menuitem has an accessible name
Ensure ARIA attributes are used as described in the specification of the element's role
Ensure elements do not use deprecated roles
Ensure aria-hidden="true" is not present on the document body.
Ensure aria-hidden elements are not focusable nor contain focusable elements
Ensure every ARIA input field has an accessible name
Ensure ARIA attributes are not prohibited for an element's role
Ensure elements with ARIA roles have all required ARIA attributes
Ensure aria-roledescription is only used on elements with an implicit or explicit role
Ensure all elements with a role attribute use a valid value
Ensure every ARIA toggle field has an accessible name
Ensure every ARIA tooltip node has an accessible name
Ensure all ARIA attributes have valid values
Ensure attributes that begin with aria- are valid ARIA attributes
Ensure buttons have discernible text
Ensure every id attribute value used in ARIA and in labels is unique
Ensure <iframe> and <frame> elements contain a unique title attribute
Ensure <iframe> and <frame> elements have an accessible name
Ensure input buttons have discernible text
Ensure <input type="image"> elements have alternative text
Ensure every form element has a label
Ensure links have discernible text
Ensure interactive controls are not nested as they are not always announced by screen readers or can cause focus problems for assistive technologies
Ensure select element has an accessible name
Ensure summary elements have discernible text

commonMistakes: Building buttons, checkboxes, or tabs from <div>s with no role and no ARIA state — use native elements or add role plus aria-*.
Buttons, links, or inputs with no accessible name (axe button-name, link-name, label).
A custom toggle that never updates aria-checked or aria-expanded when its state changes.
Invalid or unsupported ARIA attributes and roles (axe aria-allowed-attr, aria-roles).
An icon-only button whose only content is an SVG with no label.

criteria.title: Status Messages
criteria.officialText: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.
criteria.plainEnglish: Status messages—'Item added to cart', 'Search returned 12 results', a save confirmation—must be announced to screen readers without moving focus. Use an ARIA live region (aria-live, or role="status"/"alert") so assistive tech reads the update while the user keeps working.
criteria.whyItMatters: Screen reader users miss important feedback—like 'changes saved' or 'invalid coupon'—when status updates appear visually but aren't announced through a live region.
criteria.quickCheck: Trigger status updates (add to cart, search, save) and confirm they live in an aria-live region (role="status"/"alert"); verify a screen reader announces them without focus moving.

codeExamples.label: Status announced via a live region
codeExamples.code:
{/* Announced without moving focus */}
{statusMessage}

codeExamples.label: Status updates but is never announced
codeExamples.code:
{/* No live region — screen readers stay silent */}
{statusMessage}

commonMistakes: Showing a toast or status text with no aria-live region, so screen readers stay silent.
Moving focus to the status message instead of announcing it via a live region.
Using role="alert" for non-urgent updates (it interrupts) instead of role="status".
Inserting the live region and its text at the same time, so the change isn't announced.
